blocks:
Ayahs

طه (١)
Ta Ha! (1)
مَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ لِتَشْقَىٰٓ (٢)
We did not send down to you the Quran that you should be miserable, (2)
إِلَّا تَذْكِرَةً لِّمَن يَخْشَىٰ (٣)
but only as an admonition to him who fears [his Lord]. (3)
تَنزِيلًا مِّمَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ ٱلْعُلَى (٤)
A sending down [of the Revelation] from Him who created the earth and the lofty heavens (4)
ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱسْتَوَىٰ (٥)
—the All-beneficent, settled on the Throne. (5)
لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا وَمَا تَحْتَ ٱلثَّرَىٰ (٦)
To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and whatever is between them, and whatever is under the ground. (6)
وَإِن تَجْهَرْ بِٱلْقَوْلِ فَإِنَّهُۥ يَعْلَمُ ٱلسِّرَّ وَأَخْفَى (٧)
Whether you speak loudly [or in secret tones,] He indeed knows the secret and what is still more hidden. (7)
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ (٨)
Allah—there is no god except Him—to Him belong the Best Names. (8)

(Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to serve as a reminder by way of warning; the verses of warning and intimidation clearly predominate over the verses of glad tidings. It contains stories that conclude with the destruction of tyrants and those who denied the signs of Allah. It also includes clear arguments that compel the intellect to acknowledge His monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and to respond to the call of truth. It concludes by describing the terrors of the Hour, the stations of the Resurrection, the wretched state of the criminals, and the loss of the wrongdoers that man will face.

The verses open—as appears from the context—with a form of consolation for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), advising him not to exhaust his noble self in trying to compel the people to accept his call contained in the Quran. It was not sent down to be a source of distress or burden for him; rather, it is a divine revelation (تنزيل إلهي) to remind people of Allah and His signs, in the hope that their innate disposition toward fear (غريزة خشيتهم) might awaken, leading them to remember, believe in Him, and become God-conscious (يتقوا). His duty is nothing but delivery (التبليغ) of the message. If they fear and remember, then well and good; otherwise, they will be covered by the overwhelming punishment of extirpation, or they will be returned to their Lord, where the evil consequence of their wrongdoing and transgression will overtake them, and their deeds will be repaid in full, without them being able to frustrate Allah the Glorified through their tyranny and denial.

The context of the Surah's verses suggests it is Meccan. Some traditions mention that His saying: فَاصْبِرْ عَلى ما يَقُولُونَ — "So be patient with what they say" (20:130) is Medinan, and others state that His saying: لا تَمُدَّنَّ عَيْنَيْكَ إِلى ما مَتَّعْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْهُمْ — "Do not extend your glance toward what We have provided certain groups of them" (20:131) is Medinan. However, there is no linguistic evidence for any of that.

Among the most luminous verses in the Surah is His saying, the Exalted: اللَّهُ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَهُ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَى — "Allah—there is no god except Him. To Him belong the Best Names" (20:8).

His saying, the Exalted: طه مَا أَنْزَلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لِتَشْقَى — "Ta Ha. We did not send down to you the Quran that you should be miserable" (20:1-2). Ta Ha (طه) are two of the disjointed letters (الحروف المقطعة) with which the Surah opens, like the other disjointed letters that open their respective surahs, such as Alif Lam Mim (الم) and Alif Lam Ra (الر) and their likes. Various matters have been narrated from a group of exegetes regarding the meaning of these two letters, which exegetical inquiry should be too dignified to record or delve into. We shall allude to them in the upcoming "Discussion on Traditions," Allah willing.

Miserable (الشقاوة) is the opposite of happiness (السعادة). Al-Raghib said: "Miserable (الشقاوة), like happiness (السعادة), is relative. Just as happiness is originally of two types: otherworldly happiness and worldly happiness, and worldly happiness is of three types: psychological, physical, and external; so too is misery divided into these types." He continues: "Some have said: Misery (الشقاء) is sometimes used in the sense of fatigue (التعب), as in 'I was miserable in such-and-all.' Every misery is fatigue, but not every fatigue is misery; thus, fatigue is more general than misery." [End of quote]. Thus, the meaning is: We did not send down the Quran for you to exhaust yourself in the path of its delivery by burdening yourself with forcing people to accept it.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا تَذْكِرَةً لِمَنْ يَخْشَى تَنْزِيلاً مِمَّنْ خَلَقَ الْأَرْضَ وَالسَّماواتِ الْعُلى — "but as a reminder for him who stands in awe, a revelation from Him who created the earth and the high heavens" (20:3-4). The reminder (التذكرة) is the act of producing remembrance (الذكر) in one who has forgotten something. Since man attains the universal realities of religion through his primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — such as the existence of the Exalted, His uniqueness in the necessity of His existence, His divinity (ألوهيته), His lordship (ربوبيته), prophethood, the return (المعاد), and other matters, these were things deposited in the primordial nature (الفطرة). However, man's clinging to the earth, his turning toward the world, and his preoccupation with its fleeting ornaments—a preoccupation that leaves no room in his heart—made him forget what was deposited in his primordial nature (الفطرة). Thus, the presentation of these realities was to draw his soul toward them and to serve as a reminder (تذكرة) for him after his forgetfulness.

It is well known that this is actually a turning away (إعراض), and it is only called forgetfulness (نسيان) by way of a certain figurative consideration (عناية), which is their shared effect: the lack of concern for the matter. To dispel this forgetfulness caused by following whims and devotion to the world, there must be something that wrenches the soul away and pushes it toward accepting the truth. This is awe (الخشية) and fear of the consequence of heedlessness and the evil of unbridled indulgence, so that the reminder (التذكرة) may find its place and benefit its possessor in following the truth.

From the preceding explanation, the reason for qualifying the reminder (التذكرة) with His saying: لِمَنْ يَخْشى — "for him who stands in awe" becomes clear. The meaning of "him who stands in awe" is one whose nature is such that he is prepared for awe to manifest in his heart if he hears the word of truth; so when the reminder reaches him, awe manifests within him, and he believes and becomes God-conscious.

The exception in His saying: إِلَّا تَذْكِرَةً — "but as a reminder" is a disconnected exception (استثناء منقطع)—according to what they have said—and the meaning is: We did not send down the Quran to you that you should exhaust yourself with it, but rather that it be a reminder by which he whose nature is to stand in awe may remember, and thus fear, believe in Allah, and be God-conscious.

The natural flow of the context requires that "reminder" (تذكرة) be a verbal noun (مصدر) acting as an active participle and an object of purpose for His saying: ما أَنْزَلْنا — "We did not send down." It also requires that His saying: تَنْزِيلاً — "a revelation" be in the sense of a passive participle, acting as a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the pronoun in "reminder" (تذكرة) referring back to the Quran. The meaning is: We did not send down the Quran to you that you should exhaust yourself with it, but rather to remind the awe-stricken with a divine speech revealed from Him.

His saying: تَنْزِيلاً مِمَّنْ خَلَقَ الْأَرْضَ وَالسَّماواتِ الْعُلى — "a revelation from Him who created the earth and the high heavens." The "high" (العلى) is the plural of ulya (عليا), the feminine of a'la (أعلى), like fudla (فضلى) and fudal (فضل). The choice of the creation of the earth and the heavens as the relative clause (صلة) for the relative pronoun, and as an explanation for the ambiguity of the One who revealed it, is due to its suitability to the meaning of revelation (التنزيل), which is only completed by a high and a low that serve as the starting point and the destination for this movement. They were specifically mentioned without what is between them because no purpose pertains to what is between them here; rather, the purpose is to clarify the starting point and destination of the revelation. This differs from His saying: لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُما — "To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and whatever is between them" (20:6), where the purpose is to show the all-encompassing nature of His ownership (الملك) over everything.

His saying, the Exalted: الرَّحْمنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوى — "The All-beneficent, settled on the Throne" (20:5). This is a new sentence (استئناف) in which the issue of the monotheism of lordship (توحيد الربوبية) is mentioned, which is the core purpose of the call and the reminder. This is covered in four verses: الرَّحْمنُ — "The All-beneficent" until His saying: لَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى — "To Him belong the Best Names."

It has already been mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يُغْشِي اللَّيْلَ النَّهارَ — "then He settled on the Throne. He draws the night's cover over the day" (7:54), that "settling on the Throne" (الاستواء على العرش) is a metaphor for encompassing the kingdom and taking the reins of the management of affairs (تدبير الأمور). In relation to Him, the Exalted—in a manner that suits the court of His majesty and holiness—it is the manifestation of His authority over the cosmos and the establishment of His dominion over all things by managing their affairs and rectifying their conditions.

His settling on the Throne necessitates that His ownership encompasses everything and that His management extends over all things: celestial and terrestrial, the magnificent and the minute, the momentous and the trivial. Thus, He, the Exalted, is the Lord of everything, unique in lordship (الربوبية), for we mean by "Lord" (الرب) nothing but the owner of a thing who manages its affairs. Therefore, the discourse on settling on the Throne was followed by the discourse on His ownership of everything and His knowledge of everything, which serves as the rationale and argument for the aforementioned settling.

It is evident that "the All-Beneficent" (الرَّحْمنُ) — the one whose mercy is vast and encompasses all creation — which is an intensive form of mercy (الرحمة) meaning the bestowal of existence and management, and which implies abundance, is more suitable for "settling" (الاستواء) than other names and attributes. Therefore, it was specifically mentioned among them.

It has become clear from the preceding that "the All-Beneficent" (الرَّحْمنُ) is the subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is "settled" (اسْتَوى), and "on the Throne" (عَلَى الْعَرْشِ) is related to His saying "settled" (اسْتَوى). The intent is to clarify the settling on the Throne. This is also what is understood from other verses, as the discourse on settling on the Throne is repeated in verses such as: ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يُغْشِي اللَّيْلَ النَّهارَ — "then He settled on the Throne. He draws the night's cover over the day" (7:54), and His saying: ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ — "then He settled on the Throne, directing the command" (10:3), and His saying: ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ — "then He settled on the Throne. You do not have besides Him any guardian" (32:4), and His saying: ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يَعْلَمُ ما يَلِجُ فِي الْأَرْضِ — "then He settled on the Throne. He knows whatever enters the earth" (57:4), and others.

By this, the invalidity of what has been attributed to some becomes clear: that His saying "The All-Beneficent is on the Throne" is a subject and predicate, then His saying "settled" (اسْتَوى) is a verb whose subject is "whatever is in the heavens," and His saying "to Him" (لَهُ) is related to His saying "settled" (اسْتَوى), meaning the settling of everything for Him, the Exalted, by their proceeding according to His will and their submission to His command.

We have discussed the meaning of the Throne (العرش) extensively in the commentary on verse 54 of Surah al-A'raf in the eighth volume of this book, and some points specific to this context will follow in the "Discussion on Traditions," Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُما وَما تَحْتَ الثَّرى — "To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and whatever is between them, and whatever is under the ground" (20:6).

The ground (الثرى), as has been said, is moist soil or soil in general. Thus, what is meant by "under the ground" (تَحْتَ الثَّرى) is what is inside the earth beneath the soil. What remains for "whatever is on the earth" is what is on its surface, its parts, and what lives upon it that we know and perceive, such as humans and various types of animals and plants, as well as what we do not know or perceive.

If ownership encompasses what is in the heavens and the earth, including their parts, it encompasses the heavens and the earth themselves, for a thing is nothing but its parts.

In this verse, one of the two pillars of lordship (الربوبية) has been explained, which is ownership (الملك); for the meaning of lordship, as mentioned just now, is ownership and management (التدبير).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ تَجْهَرْ بِالْقَوْلِ فَإِنَّهُ يَعْلَمُ السِّرَّ وَأَخْفى — "Whether you speak aloud, He indeed knows the secret and what is yet more hidden" (20:7). Speaking aloud (الجهر بالقول) is raising the voice with it, and whispering is its opposite. The Exalted said: وَأَسِرُّوا قَوْلَكُمْ أَوِ اجْهَرُوا بِهِ — "Whether you keep your discourse secret or make it public" (67:13). The secret (السر) is the speech concealed within the soul. His saying: وَأَخْفى — "and what is yet more hidden" is a superlative (أفعل التفضيل) derived from hiddenness (الخفاء), as indicated by the context of progression (الترقي) in the verse. No heed should be paid to the one who says that "more hidden" (أَخْفى) is a past tense verb whose subject is a pronoun referring to Him, the Exalted, meaning: He knows the secret and He has hidden its knowledge. Furthermore, the indefinite form of "more hidden" (أَخْفى) emphasizes the hiddenness.

The mention of speaking aloud first, then the affirmation of knowledge of what is more subtle than it—which is the secret—and then the progression to what is even more hidden, indicates that the intent is to affirm knowledge of everything. The meaning is: Whether you speak aloud and announce what you intend—and it seems "speech" (القول) refers to what is in the mind since its manifestation is usually through speech—or you keep it secret within yourself and conceal it, or it is even more hidden than that, such that it is hidden even from your own self, Allah indeed knows it.

The basis is the division of speech into that which is spoken aloud, the secret, and the more hidden, and the affirmation of knowledge of all of them. Then, the affirmation of knowledge of the secret and the more hidden was placed in the position of the second part of the division and the response, for the sake of brevity. Thus, it indicates the response for both parts of the division together, and conveys the meaning of "all the more so" (الأولوية) with the briefest expression. It is as if it were said: "If you ask about His knowledge of what you speak aloud, He knows it; and how could He not know it when He knows the secret and what is even more hidden than it?" This is a subtle literary device in speech.

Some have mentioned that "the secret" (السر) refers to what you whisper to someone else without raising your voice, and "what is more hidden" (أَخْفى) refers to what occurs to your mind. This which he mentioned is true regarding whispering, but speech is only called a secret because of its concealment in the soul; thus, the reliance is on the meaning we previously provided.

In any case, the verse affirms His knowledge, the Exalted, of everything, whether manifest or hidden. In its mention of knowledge following the settling on the Throne, it is similar to His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ اسْتَوى عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يَعْلَمُ ما يَلِجُ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَما يَخْرُجُ مِنْها — "then He settled on the Throne. He knows whatever enters the earth and whatever emerges from it" (57:4). It is well known that His knowledge, the Exalted, of what occurs in His kingdom and what happens in the seat of His authority necessitates His pleasure with it and His permission, and from another perspective, His will (مشيئته) for this ongoing system; and this is management (التدبير).

Thus, the verse affirms the universality of management, just as the previous verse affirmed the universality of ownership. The sum of their meanings is ownership and management, which is the meaning of absolute lordship (الربوبية المطلقة). Therefore, the two verses are in the position of providing the rationale by which His absolute lordship is established.

His saying, the Exalted: اللَّهُ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَهُ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَى — "Allah—there is no god except Him. To Him belong the Best Names" (20:8). This is like a conclusion to the preceding verses. Therefore, it would be most appropriate for the Name of Majesty (Allah) to be the predicate of a deleted subject, the estimation being: "This One mentioned in the previous verses is Allah, there is no god except Him..." etc. However, considering the independence of the verse and the comprehensiveness of its content, it is closer to consider the Name of Majesty as the subject (مبتدأ), His saying: لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ — "there is no god except Him" as its predicate (خبر), and His saying: لَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى — "To Him belong the Best Names" as a second predicate.

In any case, His saying: اللَّهُ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ — "Allah—there is no god except Him" can be justified by what was established in the previous verses regarding His uniqueness in absolute lordship, and it can also be justified by His saying after it: لَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى — "To Him belong the Best Names."

As for the first, it is because the meaning of "God" (الإله) in the word of monotheism (كلمة التهليل) is either the Worshipped (المعبود) or the One rightfully worshipped (المعبود بالحق). Thus, the meaning of the speech is: "Allah—there is no rightful worshipped one other than Him," or "no rightfully worshipped one exists other than Him." Being worshipped (المعبودية) is among the affairs of lordship (الربوبية) and its consequences, for worship (العبادة) is a type of representation and enactment of servitude (العبودية) and being owned (المملوكية), and a manifestation of need for Him. Therefore, it is necessary that the Worshipped be the owner of His worshipper and the manager of his affairs—that is, a Lord (رب) for him. Since He, the Exalted, is the Lord of everything and there is no lord besides Him, He is the Worshipped, and there is no worshipped one besides Him.

As for the second, it is because worship of someone is due to one of three traits: either out of hope for the good that the worshipped possesses, thus worshipping him out of desire for the good he has to attain it; or out of fear of the evil that lies in turning away from him and neglecting his command; or because he is worthy of worship and submission.

Allah, the Glorified, is the Owner of all good; nothing owns any good except what He has given it to own, and He remains the Owner even of what He has given, and the One powerful over what He has empowered others over. He is the Bestower of blessings, the Gracious, the Giver of life, the Healer, the Provider, the All-Forgiving, the Most Merciful, the Self-Sufficient, the All-Mighty. To Him belongs every name that carries the meaning of good. Thus, He, the Glorified, is the One deserving of worship out of hope for the good He possesses, to the exclusion of others.

Allah, the Glorified, is the All-Mighty, the Subduer (القاهر) whom nothing can withstand. He is the Avenger, the One of great might, the Severe in punishment. No one faces evil from anyone except by His permission. Thus, He is the One deserving to be worshipped out of fear of His wrath, should one not submit to His greatness and majesty.

Allah, the Glorified, is the only One worthy of worship because a thing's worthiness of having others submit to it for its own sake is only due to perfection (كمال). Perfection alone is that before which deficiency—which is inseparable from submission—submits. This is either beauty (جمال) to which the soul is irresistibly drawn, or majesty (جلال) before which the intellect is humbled and the heart is lost. To Him, the Glorified, belongs all beauty, and there is no beauty except that it is a sign of His beauty. To Him, the Glorified, belongs all majesty, and everything besides Him is His sign. Thus, Allah—there is no god except Him, and no worshipped one besides Him, because to Him belong the Best Names.

The meaning of this is that every name that is the best of the names that are its counterparts belongs to Him, the Exalted. To clarify: describing the name as "best" (الحسن) indicates that what is meant by it is what is called in the terminology of morphology a "description" (صفة), such as the active participle and the adjectival noun, rather than the "name" in the sense of a proper noun for the Essence (علم الذات). This is because the function of proper nouns is merely to point to essences, while being described as "good" or "ugly" is the property of descriptions due to their inclusion of meanings, such as "the just," "the unjust," "the knowing," and "the ignorant." Thus, what is meant by "the Best Names" (الأسماء الحسنى) are the words indicating beautiful descriptive meanings that reach the pinnacle of beauty, such as "the Living," "the All-Knowing," and "the All-Powerful." Often, "naming" (التسمية) is applied to "describing" (التوصيف); the Exalted said: قُلْ سَمُّوهُمْ — "Say, 'Name them'" (13:33), meaning: describe them.

This is also indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَى فَادْعُوهُ بِهَا وَذَرُوا الَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِي أَسْمَائِهِ — "To Allah belong the Best Names, so supplicate Him by them, and abandon those who blaspheme His names" (7:180), meaning: those who deviate from the truth to falsehood and apply names to Him that do not befit the court of His holiness.

Thus, what is meant by "the Best Names" are those that indicate descriptive meanings, such as "the God," "the Living," "the All-Knowing," and "the All-Powerful," rather than the Name of Majesty (Allah), which is the proper noun for the Essence. Furthermore, names are divided into ugly ones, such as "the unjust," "the tyrannical," and "the ignorant"; and good ones, such as "the just" and "the knowing." Good names are further divided into those that contain some perfection even if not free from a taint of deficiency and contingency (الإمكان), such as "beautiful of appearance," "moderate of stature," and "curly-haired"; and those that contain perfection without any taint, such as "the Living," "the All-Knowing," and "the All-Powerful," by stripping their meanings of the taints of matter and composition. These are the "Best Names" due to their innocence from deficiency and flaw, and they are the ones that befit being applied to Him, the Exalted, and Him being described by them.

This is not restricted to one name over another; rather, every name that is "best" belongs to Him, the Exalted, because of the plural form (الأسماء) defined by the article (ال) which implies exhaustiveness (الاستغراق) in His saying, the Exalted: لَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى — "To Him belong the Best Names." The advancement of the predicate (لَهُ) implies exclusivity (الحصر); thus, all of them belong to Him alone.

The meaning of them belonging to Him, the Exalted, is that He, the Exalted, owns them by His essence. That which is found of them in others is through His granting of ownership, according to what He wills, as indicated by the course of the following verses which follow the course of exclusivity, such as His saying: هُوَ الْحَيُّ لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ — "He is the Living One, there is no god except Him" (40:65), and His saying: وَهُوَ الْعَلِيمُ الْقَدِيرُ — "and He is the All-knowing, the All-powerful" (30:54), and His saying: هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ — "He is the All-hearing, the All-seeing" (40:56), and His saying: أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً — "that power belongs entirely to Allah" (2:165), and His saying: فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً — "for all might belongs to Allah" (4:139), and His saying: وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَيْءٍ مِنْ عِلْمِهِ إِلَّا بِمَا شَاءَ — "and they do not comprehend anything of His knowledge except what He wishes" (2:255), and others.

There is no problem in generalizing His ownership to all His names and attributes, even those that are identical to His essence (عين ذاته), such as "the Living," "the All-Knowing," and "the All-Powerful," and such as life, knowledge, and power. For a thing is sometimes attributed to itself by way of ownership, as in His saying, the Exalted: رَبِّ إِنِّي لَا أَمْلِكُ إِلَّا نَفْسِي — "My Lord! I have no control except over myself" (5:25).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma': "Regarding His saying: ما أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لِتَشْقى — 'We did not send down to you the Quran that you should be miserable.' It is narrated that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to raise one of his feet in prayer to increase his fatigue, so Allah the Exalted sent down: طه ما أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لِتَشْقى — 'Ta Ha. We did not send down to you the Quran that you should be miserable.' This was also narrated from Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام)."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from 'Abd ibn Humayd and ibn al-Mundhir from al-Rabi' ibn Anas, and also from ibn Mardawayh from ibn 'Abbas.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain of narration to Abu Basir from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (عليهما السلام), they said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to stand on his toes when he prayed until they became swollen. So Allah, Blessed and Exalted, sent down: طه — which in the language of Tayy means 'O Muhammad' — ما أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لِتَشْقى إِلَّا تَذْكِرَةً لِمَنْ يَخْشى — 'We did not send down to you the Quran that you should be miserable, but as a reminder for him who stands in awe.'"

I say: A similar meaning was narrated in al-Kafi with its chain to Abu Basir from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), and in al-Ihtijaj from Musa ibn Ja'far from his fathers from 'Ali (عليه السلام). This meaning was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from ibn al-Mundhir, ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi from ibn 'Abbas.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, ibn Mardawayh narrated from 'Ali who said: "When there was revealed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): يا أَيُّهَا الْمُزَّمِّلُ قُمِ اللَّيْلَ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً — 'O you wrapped up in your mantle! Stand vigil through the night, except a little' (73:1-2), he stood the entire night until his feet became swollen. He began to raise one foot and put down the other. Then Gabriel descended to him and said: طه — meaning 'Tread (طه) the earth with both your feet, O Muhammad' — ما أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لِتَشْقى — 'We did not send down to you the Quran that you should be miserable,' and He revealed: فَاقْرَؤُا ما تَيَسَّرَ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ — 'so recite as much of the Quran as is feasible' (73:20)."

I say: What is most likely and consistent with reason is that this narration is the basis of the story: that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stood on his feet in prayer until they became swollen, then he began to raise one foot and put down the other, or he stood on the balls of his feet or his toes. Each of the narrations mentioned part of the story as the occasion of revelation (سبب النزول), even if the wording of some narrations does not fully support this.

However, two matters remain regarding the narration:

First: The correspondence of the verses, given their context, to the story is somewhat obscure.

Second: What is in the narration: "He said: Ta Ha, meaning tread the earth with both your feet, O Muhammad," and its like in the narration of al-Qummi: "So Allah revealed: Ta Ha — in the language of Tayy, 'O Muhammad' — We did not send down to you the Quran that you should be miserable." This would mean that Ta Ha is a verbal sentence composed of an imperative verb from wata'a yata'u (to tread) and its object is a feminine pronoun referring to the earth; i.e., "Tread the earth and place both your feet upon it, and do not raise one and put down the other."

In that case, the objection is that this conclusion does not align with the beginning of the narration. The beginning states that he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was raising one foot and putting down the other in prayer due to the swelling of his feet, seeking thereby to alleviate the pain of the foot he was raising, so he might rest for a moment and occupy himself with his Lord without distraction. Based on this, the removal of hardship and fatigue from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as suits the situation, would be by commanding him to reduce the prayer or lighten the standing, not by commanding him to place both feet on the ground, which would increase his fatigue and intensify his pain. Thus, the saying طه (Tread) would not harmonize with His saying: ما أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْقُرْآنَ لِتَشْقى — "We did not send down to you the Quran that you should be miserable." Perhaps the saying "meaning the earth with your feet" is from the words of the narrator and is a transmission of the meaning.

Furthermore, it contradicts the transmitted recitations which establish that Ta Ha are two disjointed letters with no conventional linguistic meaning, like the other disjointed letters that open several Quranic surahs.

A group of people mentioned that the meaning of Ta Ha is "O man." Some then said it is in the Nabataean language; it was also said to be Ethiopic, Hebrew, Syriac, the dialect of 'Ukl, or the dialect of 'Akk. It was also said to be the language of Quraysh. Al-Zamakhshari considered it possible that it is the dialect of 'Akk, originally being ya hadha (O this one), where the ya was changed to ta and the dha was deleted for brevity, becoming Ta Ha. It was also said to mean "O so-and-so." A group recited Ta Ha with a fatha on the ta and a sukun on the ha, as if it were an imperative from wata'a yata'u with the ha for pausing (هاء السكت). It was also said to be one of the names of Allah. No weight is given to any of these opinions, and there is no benefit in delving deeply into them.

Yes, it has been narrated from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) as in Ruh al-Ma'ani, and from Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام) as in Ma'ani al-Akhbar, with its chain to al-Thawri: that Ta Ha is one of the names of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), just as it has been narrated in other traditions that Ya Sin is one of his names. Both names were narrated together in al-Durr al-Manthur from ibn Mardawayh from Sayf from Abu Ja'far.

Since it was a heavenly naming, he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was not called by it nor known by it before the revelation of the Quran. Nor does Ta Ha have a descriptive meaning in the language. There is no sense in naming him with an improvised proper noun (علم ارتجالي) that has no meaning other than the essence, given the existence of his name and his fame by it. The truth regarding the disjointed letters at the openings of the surahs is that they carry symbolic meanings (معاني رمزية) that Allah conveyed to His Messenger. Since Surah Ta Ha begins by addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): طه ما أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ — "Ta Ha. We did not send down to you..." etc., just as Surah Ya Sin does: يس وَالْقُرْآنِ الْحَكِيمِ إِنَّكَ لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ — "Ya Sin. By the wise Quran, you are indeed one of the apostles" (36:1-3), unlike other surahs opened with disjointed letters, it appears that the meaning symbolized by the disjointed letters at the opening of these two surahs is a matter pertaining to his person (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), realized in him specifically. Thus, it was a description of his inner personality, exclusive to him, and so it became one of his names (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). When he was addressed and it was said "Ta Ha" or "Ya Sin," the meaning was "the one addressed by Ta Ha or Ya Sin," and then it became a proper noun through frequent usage.

This is what we have been able to provide as a justification for the narration. Its category would be that of naming someone with phrases like Ta'abbata Sharran (he carried evil under his arm), and like the poet's saying:

I am the son of Jala and the climber of high mountain passes; When I put on the turban, you will recognize me.

He means: "I am the son of the one about whom people frequently said 'He appeared (jala), he appeared,' until he was named Jala."

In the Ihtijaj of al-Tabarsi, from al-Hasan ibn Rashid, who said: "Abu al-Hasan Musa (عليه السلام) was asked about the saying of Allah: الرَّحْمنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوى — 'The All-beneficent, settled on the Throne.' He said: 'He took mastery (استولى) over what is minute and what is magnificent.'"

In al-Tawhid, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Mazin: "Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام) was asked about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: الرَّحْمنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوى — 'The All-beneficent, settled on the Throne.' He said: 'He is equal (استوى) from everything—so nothing is closer to Him than anything else.'"

I say: Al-Qummi also narrated it in his Tafsir from him (عليه السلام). It was also narrated in al-Tawhid with its chain to Muqatil ibn Sulayman from him (عليه السلام). It was also narrated in al-Kafi and al-Tawhid with the chain to 'Abd al-Rahman ibn al-Hajjaj from him (عليه السلام), and they added: "Nothing distant is far from Him, and nothing near is close to Him—He is equal (استوى) from everything."

In al-Ihtijaj, from 'Ali (عليه السلام) in a tradition regarding: الرَّحْمنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوى — "The All-beneficent, settled on the Throne." He said: "It means His management (تدبيره) became established and His command became exalted."

I say: The interpretation provided in these three narrations is an interpretation of the verse as a whole, not just of His saying "settled" (اسْتَوى). Otherwise, His saying "The All-Beneficent is on the Throne" would become a complete sentence composed of a subject and predicate, which is not supported by the context of other verses on "settling," as previously indicated.

This is supported by what is in the last narration: "and His command became exalted" after his saying: "His management became established." For it is apparent that being on the Throne is intended in the interpretation. Thus, the narrations are based on the verse being a metaphor for mastery (الاستيلاء) and the expansion of authority.

In al-Tawhid, with its chain to al-Mufaddal ibn 'Umar from Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "Whoever claims that Allah is from something, or in something, or on something, has committed polytheism (أشرك)." Then he said: "Whoever claims that Allah is from something has made Him originated (محدثا); whoever claims He is in something has claimed He is contained (محصور); and whoever claims He is on something has made Him carried (محمولا)."

Also in it, from Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام) in a long tradition, in which the questioner said: "Then what of His saying: الرَّحْمنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوى — 'The All-beneficent, settled on the Throne'?" Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام) said: "By that He described Himself. Likewise, He is mastering (مستول) the Throne, distinct from His creation—without the Throne carrying Him, nor the Throne containing Him, nor the Throne being a place of distinction for Him. But we say: He is the Carrier of the Throne and the Sustainer of the Throne, and we say regarding that what He said: وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ — 'His seat embraces the heavens and the earth' (2:255).

Thus, we affirm of the Throne and the Seat what He affirmed—and we deny that the Throne or the Seat contain Him—and that He, Mighty and Majestic, is in need of a place or anything of what He created—rather, His creation is in need of Him."

I say: His saying (عليه السلام): "Thus, we affirm of the Throne and the Seat what He affirmed... etc." is an allusion to the method of the People of the House (عليه السلام) in interpreting the ambiguous verses (الآيات المتشابهة) of the Quran—those pertaining to His names, attributes, acts, and signs that lie beyond the senses. This is done by referring them back to the clear verses (المحكمات) and denying what the clear verses deny regarding His court, the Exalted, while affirming what is established by the verse, which is the original meaning (أصل المعنى) stripped of the taints of deficiency and contingency that the clear verses have denied.

The Throne (العرش) is the station from which the reins of commands and decrees issued by the King begin and to which they return. It is a vaulted, elevated seat with legs, made of wood or metal, upon which a king sits. However, the clear verses (المحكمات), such as His saying, the Exalted: لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ — "Nothing is like Him" (41:11), and His saying: سُبْحانَ اللهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ — "Clear is Allah of what they describe" (37:159), indicate the negation of body and its properties from Him, the Exalted. Thus, one denies from the Throne that He described for Himself in His saying: الرَّحْمنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوى — "The All-beneficent, settled on the Throne" (20:5) and His saying: وَرَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ — "and the Lord of the Great Throne" (23:86), its being a seat made of such-and-such material in a specific form. What remains is the original meaning: that it is the station from which the decrees governing the cosmic system are issued, and it is one of the levels of knowledge external to the Essence.

The criterion for knowing what we have called "the original meaning" is that it is the meaning whose preservation allows the name to remain; in other words, the validity of the name revolves around it, even if the instances (المصاديق) change and the specificities differ.

For example, the lamp (السراج) first appeared as a tool for illumination in the darkness of night. Its instance then was a vessel in which a wick was placed in a fatty substance, and its tip was lit, burning by drawing the fat and illuminating its surroundings. Then the name was transferred to things like candles and oil lamps, and it continued to move from one instance to another until it settled today in the electric lamp, which possesses nothing of the material or form of the initial instance at all, except that it is a tool for illumination in darkness. By virtue of this, it is truly called a lamp.

Similar is the weapon (السلاح), which when it first appeared was a name for something like a copper axe or a shield, for example. Today, it is truly applied to things like cannons and atomic bombs. This type of transformation and evolution has spread to many of the means of life and actions that man employs in his existence.

In short, the Companions did not speak about matters of religious knowledge other than the legal rulings—matters pertaining to His names, attributes, acts, and others—except that they would deny the implications of anthropomorphism (التشبيه) based on the verses of transcendence (التنزيه) and remain silent regarding the affirmative meaning that remains after the negation. They would say, for example, regarding His saying: الرَّحْمنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوى — "The All-beneficent, settled on the Throne," that "settling" in the sense of a body's establishment in a place by relying upon it is negated from Him, the Exalted. As for what is meant by "settling," Allah knows best His intent, and the matter is delegated (مفوض) to Him. A consensus among them has been claimed on this; rather, some have said that the people of the first three centuries of the Hijra were unanimous on delegation (التفويض), which is the negation of the implications of anthropomorphism and silence regarding the investigation of the original intent.

However, this is refuted by the method of the Imams of the People of the House (عليه السلام) transmitted from them, which is both affirmation and negation together, and deep investigation into the realities of religion, rather than negation stripped of affirmation. The proof for this is the vast number of traditions preserved from them, which no one can deny except a stubborn person.

Rather, what was narrated from Umm Salama (رضي الله عنها) regarding the meaning of "settling" (الاستواء), that she said: "The settling is not unknown, the 'how' is not conceivable—affirming it is faith and denying it is disbelief," indicates that she held this view. Had she held what is attributed to the Companions, she would have said: "The settling is unknown, the 'how' is not conceivable," etc.

Yes, the majority of the Companions, the Successors, and their followers among the predecessors (السلف) followed this path. Al-Ghazali attributed it to the four Imams: Abu Hanifa, Malik, al-Shafi'i, and Ahmad; and to al-Bukhari, al-Tirmidhi, and Abu Dawud al-Sijistani among the authors of the Sahih collections, and to several of the prominent predecessors.

What led them to remain silent regarding affirmation—as mentioned by a group—was that the meaning remaining after the negation contradicts the literal meaning of the word, and thus would be part of the interpretation (التأويل) whose pursuit Allah forbade in His saying: وَابْتِغاءَ تَأْوِيلِهِ وَما يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُ إِلَّا اللهُ — "and seeking its interpretation. But no one knows its interpretation except Allah" (3:7), based on pausing at "except Allah." Some even went further to forbid interpretation (التفسير) entirely, saying—as quoted by al-Alusi—that whoever interprets (فسر) has performed ta'wil (أول), and whoever does not interpret has not performed ta'wil, because ta'wil is interpretation.

It has already been explained in the commentary on the verse of the clear and ambiguous (المحكم والمتشابه) in Surah Al 'Imran that the ta'wil which is mentioned and censured is something other than the meaning that contradicts the literal sense of the word. Furthermore, referring the ambiguous to the clear and explaining it by it is not part of ta'wil at all; likewise, ta'wil is not the same as interpretation (التفسير).

Furthermore, these people, despite their caution in religious explanations pertaining to His names and attributes, the Exalted, and their restriction to negation without affirmation, did not follow this path regarding what was mentioned in the Book and the Sunnah describing His acts, the Exalted, such as the Throne, the Seat, the Veils, the Pen, the Tablet, the records of deeds, the gates of heaven, and others. Rather, they carried them according to what is familiar to us of the instances of the throne, seat, pen, tablet, and so on, even though all of them share the same criterion: the implication of that from which He, the Exalted, must be transcended, namely need and contingency.

This is because that which brought about things like the throne, seat, tablet, and pen among us humans is need. We only adopted the seat to rest upon it or to be honored by it; we adopted the throne to rest upon it, to be honored by it, to manifest uniqueness in might and greatness, and to represent thereby the designation of kingship and authority. We adopted the tablet, the pen, and writing due to the pressing need to preserve what is absent from the senses and to guard against forgetfulness, and so on.

So what is the difference between the ambiguous verses that affirm for Him, the Exalted, hearing, sight, hand, leg, pleasure, and regret—which suggest anthropomorphism leading to need and contingency—and the verses that affirm for Him a throne, a seat, an assembly, carriers for His throne, a tablet, and a pen—which also suggest need and contingency? Furthermore, what is the difference between the clear verse (المحكم) that removes ambiguity in the first group, which is His saying: لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ — "Nothing is like Him," and the clear verse that removes the ambiguity of the second group, which is His saying: وَاللهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ — "and Allah is the All-sufficient" (35:15), for example?

Indeed, Imam al-Razi mentioned as an excuse for this that if we were to open the door of ta'wil in these matters, it would lead to the permissibility of interpreting all religious knowledge and the rulings of the Sharia, which is the view of the Batiniyya. You are well aware that interpreting everything, even the legal rulings contained in the religious call and implemented among the people by the teaching and training of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), is a rejection of necessity and a contention with the self-evident. It is not of this category when evidence has established its ambiguity and there is a clear verse (آية محكمة) to which it can be referred and by which its ambiguity can be removed. Keeping it according to its literal sense to block the door of ta'wil in other clear, non-ambiguous knowledge is like killing a truth to kill a falsehood; or if you wish, say: killing a falsehood by reviving another falsehood. Moreover, you have learned that referring the ambiguous to the clear is not part of ta'wil at all.

Necessity forced some of these people to say that the creation of this magnificent luminous body, which dazzles the intellects with its greatness, in the form of a seat with legs, and its being carried and placed above the seven heavens without a sitter sitting upon it or a need calling for it, and its preservation thus for infinite ages, is only by way of grace (اللطف). Allah created it to inform the believers of it so they might believe in it through the Unseen (الغيب) and thus be rewarded in the Hereafter. Similar to it are the Tablet, the Pen, and other great signs hidden from the senses. The fallaciousness of this view is too obvious to need explanation.

After this group called the "Delegators" (المفوضة) comes the class called the "Interpreters" (المؤولة). They are those who, in interpreting the ambiguous verses of names and attributes, combine affirmation and negation. They transcend Him from the implications of need and contingency by interpreting them—in the sense of carrying them to a meaning other than the literal—to meanings that agree with the established principles of religion or the school of thought. These are divided into branches:

Among them are those who were satisfied in affirmation with the very thing they negated by proof. These are the ones who interpret the names and attributes as the negation of deficiencies. Thus, the meaning of "knowledge" for them is the absence of ignorance, and the meaning of "the All-Knowing" is the one who is not ignorant, and so on. This necessitates stripping the Transcendent Essence of the attributes of perfection. Rational proofs and the literal meanings of the Book and the Sunnah and their texts refute this; it is among the views of the Sabians that have seeped into Islam.

Among them are those who interpreted them with meanings contrary to their literal sense, from whatever the intellect or transmission allowed that does not contradict established principles. This is what they call ta'wil.

Among them are those who were satisfied with the transmitted possibilities and did not consider the intellect.

You have already learned from our previous discussions on the clear and ambiguous (المحكم والمتشابه) that interpreting the Mighty Book by other than the Book and the definitive Sunnah is part of "interpretation by opinion" (التفسير بالرأي) which is forbidden in the Book and the Sunnah.

Most of these three groups called the "Interpreters" follow the path of the predecessors called the "Delegators" regarding His acts, the Exalted, that do not pertain to attributes, by keeping them according to their literal meanings of the instances familiar to us. As for what pertains in some way to attributes, they interpret it. In His saying: الرَّحْمنُ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ اسْتَوى — "The All-beneficent, settled on the Throne," they interpret "settling" as things like mastery and being high, but they keep "the Throne"—which is an act of His, the Exalted, not pertaining to an attribute—according to its familiar literal sense, which is a body created in the form of a vaulted seat with legs. In what has been narrated through the paths of the majority (الجماعة) that Allah descends every Friday night to the lowest heaven, they interpret His descent as the descent of His mercy, and they interpret the lowest heaven as the sphere of the moon, and so on.

You have learned in what preceded that carrying the verse to other than its literal meaning has no justification and no evidence to support it. The Book was not revealed as riddles and enigmas. Furthermore, the Hadith contains the clear and the ambiguous just like the Quran. Keeping the ambiguous of the Quran according to its literal sense by relying on the literal sense of its like narrated in the Hadith is, in reality, referring the ambiguous of the Quran to the ambiguous of the Hadith, whereas we have been commanded to refer the ambiguous of the Quran to its clear (محكمه).

Furthermore, in their acting upon these narrations and making them judge over the literal meaning of the Book, there is another oversight: these are solitary reports (أخبار آحاد) that are neither mass-transmitted (متواترة) nor of definitive origin. That which is of this status requires, for acting upon it even in its authentic forms, a religious authority (حجية شرعية) through designation or endorsement. It has become clear in the science of principles (علم الأصول), with a clarity bordering on the self-evident, that there is no meaning for the authority of solitary reports in matters other than legal rulings, such as creedal knowledge (المعارف الاعتقادية) and external subjects.

Yes, the mass-transmitted report (الخبر المتواتر) and that which is surrounded by definitive indicators—such as that heard directly from the Infallible—is an authority even if it is in matters other than legal rulings, because the proof for infallibility (العصمة) is itself the proof for its truthfulness. These are all matters settled in their proper place; whoever wishes to investigate should refer there.

In the Sunan of Abu Dawud, from Jubayr ibn Muhammad ibn Jubayr ibn Mut'im, from his father, from his grandfather, who said: "A Bedouin came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, souls are exhausted, wealth is depleted, and livestock have perished, so pray for rain for us. For we seek intercession (نستشفع) through you to Allah, and we seek intercession through Allah to you.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Woe to you! Do you know what you are saying?' And the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) glorified Allah (سبح) and continued to glorify Him until that was recognized in the faces of his companions.

Then he said: 'Woe to you! Intercession is not sought through Allah to any of His creation; the status of Allah is greater than that. Woe to you! Do you know what Allah is? Indeed, Allah is above His Throne, and His Throne is above His heavens like this'—and he gestured with his fingers like a dome—'and it indeed creaks with Him like the creaking of a new saddle with the rider.'"

I say: Its text is not free from disorder. We have only cited it because it is among the most explicit reports regarding the corporeality of the Throne. There are narrations indicating that it has legs, others indicating that it has four carriers, others indicating that it is above the heavens in line with the Kaaba, and others indicating that the Seat (الكرسي) in relation to it is like a ring cast in a vast desert, and the heavens and the earth in relation to the Seat are likewise. The method of the Imams of the People of the House (عليه السلام) in interpreting such reports has already been mentioned, and we provided in the commentary of Surah al-A'raf in the eighth volume of this book the summary of their view (عليه السلام).

In Ma'ani al-Akhbar, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: "I asked Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام) about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: يَعْلَمُ السِّرَّ وَأَخْفى — 'He knows the secret and what is yet more hidden.' He said: 'The secret (السِّرَّ) is what you conceal within yourself, and what is more hidden (أَخْفى) is what occurred to your mind and then you forgot it.'"

In al-Majma', it is narrated from the two masters, al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (عليهما السلام): "The secret (السِّرَّ) is what you hid within yourself, and what is more hidden (أَخْفى) is what occurred to your mind and then you forgot it."

Ayahs

وَهَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ مُوسَىٰٓ (٩)
Did the story of Moses come to you, (9)
إِذْ رَءَا نَارًا فَقَالَ لِأَهْلِهِ ٱمْكُثُوٓا۟ إِنِّىٓ ءَانَسْتُ نَارًا لَّعَلِّىٓ ءَاتِيكُم مِّنْهَا بِقَبَسٍ أَوْ أَجِدُ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ هُدًى (١٠)
when he sighted a fire, and said to his family, ‘Wait! Indeed, I descry a fire! Maybe I will bring you a brand from it, or find some guidance at the fire.’ (10)
فَلَمَّآ أَتَىٰهَا نُودِىَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ (١١)
So when he came to it, he was called, ‘O Moses! (11)
إِنِّىٓ أَنَا۠ رَبُّكَ فَٱخْلَعْ نَعْلَيْكَ ۖ إِنَّكَ بِٱلْوَادِ ٱلْمُقَدَّسِ طُوًى (١٢)
Indeed I am your Lord! So take off your sandals. You are indeed in the sacred valley of Tuwa. (12)
وَأَنَا ٱخْتَرْتُكَ فَٱسْتَمِعْ لِمَا يُوحَىٰٓ (١٣)
I have chosen you; so listen to what is revealed. (13)
إِنَّنِىٓ أَنَا ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠ فَٱعْبُدْنِى وَأَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ لِذِكْرِىٓ (١٤)
Indeed I am Allah—there is no god except Me. So worship Me, and maintain the prayer for My remembrance. (14)
إِنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ ءَاتِيَةٌ أَكَادُ أُخْفِيهَا لِتُجْزَىٰ كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا تَسْعَىٰ (١٥)
Indeed the Hour is bound to come: I will have it hidden, so that every soul may be rewarded for its endeavour. (15)
فَلَا يَصُدَّنَّكَ عَنْهَا مَن لَّا يُؤْمِنُ بِهَا وَٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ فَتَرْدَىٰ (١٦)
So do not let yourself be distracted from it by those who do not believe in it and who follow their desires, or you will perish.’ (16)
وَمَا تِلْكَ بِيَمِينِكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ (١٧)
‘Moses, what is that in your right hand?’ (17)
قَالَ هِىَ عَصَاىَ أَتَوَكَّؤُا۟ عَلَيْهَا وَأَهُشُّ بِهَا عَلَىٰ غَنَمِى وَلِىَ فِيهَا مَـَٔارِبُ أُخْرَىٰ (١٨)
He said, ‘It is my staff. I lean on it, and with it I beat down leaves for my sheep; and I have other uses for it.’ (18)
قَالَ أَلْقِهَا يَـٰمُوسَىٰ (١٩)
He said, ‘Moses, throw it down.’ (19)
فَأَلْقَىٰهَا فَإِذَا هِىَ حَيَّةٌ تَسْعَىٰ (٢٠)
So he threw it down, and behold, it was a snake, moving swiftly. (20)
قَالَ خُذْهَا وَلَا تَخَفْ ۖ سَنُعِيدُهَا سِيرَتَهَا ٱلْأُولَىٰ (٢١)
He said, ‘Take hold of it, and do not fear. We will restore it to its former state. (21)
وَٱضْمُمْ يَدَكَ إِلَىٰ جَنَاحِكَ تَخْرُجْ بَيْضَآءَ مِنْ غَيْرِ سُوٓءٍ ءَايَةً أُخْرَىٰ (٢٢)
Now clasp your hand to your armpit: it will emerge white, without any harm—[this is yet] another sign, (22)
لِنُرِيَكَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا ٱلْكُبْرَى (٢٣)
that We may show you some of Our great signs. (23)
ٱذْهَبْ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُۥ طَغَىٰ (٢٤)
Go to Pharaoh. He has indeed rebelled.’ (24)
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱشْرَحْ لِى صَدْرِى (٢٥)
He said, ‘My Lord! Open my breast for me. (25)
وَيَسِّرْ لِىٓ أَمْرِى (٢٦)
Make my task easy for me. (26)
وَٱحْلُلْ عُقْدَةً مِّن لِّسَانِى (٢٧)
Remove the hitch from my tongue, (27)
يَفْقَهُوا۟ قَوْلِى (٢٨)
[so that] they may understand my speech. (28)
وَٱجْعَل لِّى وَزِيرًا مِّنْ أَهْلِى (٢٩)
Appoint for me a minister from my family, (29)
هَـٰرُونَ أَخِى (٣٠)
Aaron, my brother. (30)
ٱشْدُدْ بِهِۦٓ أَزْرِى (٣١)
Strengthen my back through him, (31)
وَأَشْرِكْهُ فِىٓ أَمْرِى (٣٢)
and make him my associate in my task, (32)
كَىْ نُسَبِّحَكَ كَثِيرًا (٣٣)
so that we may glorify You greatly, (33)
وَنَذْكُرَكَ كَثِيرًا (٣٤)
and remember You greatly. (34)
إِنَّكَ كُنتَ بِنَا بَصِيرًا (٣٥)
Indeed You see us best.’ (35)
قَالَ قَدْ أُوتِيتَ سُؤْلَكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ (٣٦)
He said, ‘Moses, your request has been granted! (36)
وَلَقَدْ مَنَنَّا عَلَيْكَ مَرَّةً أُخْرَىٰٓ (٣٧)
Certainly, We have done you a favour another time, (37)
إِذْ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّكَ مَا يُوحَىٰٓ (٣٨)
when We revealed to your mother whatever was to be revealed: (38)
أَنِ ٱقْذِفِيهِ فِى ٱلتَّابُوتِ فَٱقْذِفِيهِ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ فَلْيُلْقِهِ ٱلْيَمُّ بِٱلسَّاحِلِ يَأْخُذْهُ عَدُوٌّ لِّى وَعَدُوٌّ لَّهُۥ ۚ وَأَلْقَيْتُ عَلَيْكَ مَحَبَّةً مِّنِّى وَلِتُصْنَعَ عَلَىٰ عَيْنِىٓ (٣٩)
“Put him in the casket, and cast it into the river. Then the river will cast it on the bank, and he shall be picked up by an enemy of Mine and an enemy of his.” And I made you endearing, and that you might be reared under My [watchful] eyes. (39)
إِذْ تَمْشِىٓ أُخْتُكَ فَتَقُولُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكُمْ عَلَىٰ مَن يَكْفُلُهُۥ ۖ فَرَجَعْنَـٰكَ إِلَىٰٓ أُمِّكَ كَىْ تَقَرَّ عَيْنُهَا وَلَا تَحْزَنَ ۚ وَقَتَلْتَ نَفْسًا فَنَجَّيْنَـٰكَ مِنَ ٱلْغَمِّ وَفَتَنَّـٰكَ فُتُونًا ۚ فَلَبِثْتَ سِنِينَ فِىٓ أَهْلِ مَدْيَنَ ثُمَّ جِئْتَ عَلَىٰ قَدَرٍ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ (٤٠)
When your sister walked up [to Pharaoh’s palace] saying, “Shall I show you someone who will take care of him?” Then We restored you to your mother, that she might not grieve and be comforted. Then you slew a soul, whereupon We delivered you from anguish, and We tried you with various ordeals. Then you stayed for several years among the people of Midian. Then you turned up as ordained, O Moses! (40)
وَٱصْطَنَعْتُكَ لِنَفْسِى (٤١)
And I chose you for Myself.’ (41)
ٱذْهَبْ أَنتَ وَأَخُوكَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِى وَلَا تَنِيَا فِى ذِكْرِى (٤٢)
‘Go ahead, you and your brother, with My signs and do not flag in My remembrance. (42)
ٱذْهَبَآ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُۥ طَغَىٰ (٤٣)
Both of you go to Pharaoh, for he has indeed rebelled. (43)
فَقُولَا لَهُۥ قَوْلًا لَّيِّنًا لَّعَلَّهُۥ يَتَذَكَّرُ أَوْ يَخْشَىٰ (٤٤)
Speak to him in a soft manner; maybe he will take admonition or fear.’ (44)
قَالَا رَبَّنَآ إِنَّنَا نَخَافُ أَن يَفْرُطَ عَلَيْنَآ أَوْ أَن يَطْغَىٰ (٤٥)
They said, ‘Our Lord! We are indeed afraid that he will forestall us or will exceed all bounds.’ (45)
قَالَ لَا تَخَافَآ ۖ إِنَّنِى مَعَكُمَآ أَسْمَعُ وَأَرَىٰ (٤٦)
He said, ‘Do not be afraid, for I will be with the two of you, hearing and seeing [whatever happens]. (46)
فَأْتِيَاهُ فَقُولَآ إِنَّا رَسُولَا رَبِّكَ فَأَرْسِلْ مَعَنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَلَا تُعَذِّبْهُمْ ۖ قَدْ جِئْنَـٰكَ بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۖ وَٱلسَّلَـٰمُ عَلَىٰ مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ (٤٧)
So approach him and say, ‘‘We are the apostles of your Lord. Let the Children of Israel go with us, and do not torture them! We certainly bring you a sign from your Lord, and may peace be upon him who follows guidance! (47)
إِنَّا قَدْ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْنَآ أَنَّ ٱلْعَذَابَ عَلَىٰ مَن كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ (٤٨)
Indeed it has been revealed to us that punishment shall befall those who impugn us and turn their backs [on us].’’ ’ (48)

(Commentary)

This marks the beginning of the story of Moses (peace be upon him). Four chapters of his story are mentioned in this Surah: First, the selection of Moses for the messengership at Mount Tur in the valley of Tuwa, and the command for him to call Pharaoh. Second, his call—joined by his brother—to Pharaoh toward monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts, the demand to send the Children of Israel with him, the establishment of proof, and the granting of miracles. Third, his departure with the Children of Israel from Egypt, the pursuit by Pharaoh, Pharaoh's drowning, and the salvation of the Children of Israel. Fourth, the worship of the calf by the Children of Israel, the outcome of their affair, and the matter of the Samiri and his calf. The verses we have cited address the first chapter of these events.

The connection of this story to what preceded it is that it serves as a reminder (تذكرة) of monotheism and a warning (وعيد) of punishment. The story begins with the revelation of monotheism and ends with the saying of Moses: إِنَّما إِلهُكُمُ اللهُ الَّذِي لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ — "Your god is only Allah; there is no god except Him" (20:98), and mentions the destruction of Pharaoh and the expulsion of the Samiri. The preceding verses began by stating that the Quran, which contains the true call, is a reminder for those who fear, and concluded with His saying: اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى — "Allah—there is no god except Him. To Him belong the Best Names" (20:8).

His saying, the Exalted: وَهَلْ أَتاكَ حَدِيثُ مُوسى — "Has the report of Moses reached you?" (20:9). The interrogation here is for affirmation (التقرير), and "report" (الحديث) refers to the story.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ رَأى ناراً فَقالَ لِأَهْلِهِ امْكُثُوا إِنِّي آنَسْتُ ناراً — "When he sighted a fire, and said to his family, ‘Wait! Indeed, I descry a fire...’" (20:10) until the end of the verse. "Waiting" (المكث) means tarrying, and "descrying" (الإيناس) is to perceive a thing or find it; it is derived from uns (familiarity), the opposite of nufur (aversion). Thus, it is said that it means perceiving something that brings comfort, which implies a strong perception. A "brand" (القبس) is a flame taken from the tip of a stick or similar object. "Guidance" (الهدى) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — is a verbal noun used in the sense of an active participle, or it is a genitive construction where a word is omitted, meaning "one possessing guidance." In any case, it refers to someone who provides guidance.

The context of the verse and those following it testifies that this occurred during his return from Midian to Egypt with his family. They were near the valley of Tuwa at Mount Sinai on a dark, wintry night and had lost their way. When he saw a fire, he decided to go toward it; if he found someone there, he would ask for directions, otherwise, he would take a brand from the fire so they could kindle a fire to warm themselves.

In His saying, "and said to his family, ‘Wait!’" (فَقالَ لِأَهْلِهِ امْكُثُوا), there is an indication, or rather a proof, that there were others with his family. Similarly, in His saying, "Indeed, I descry a fire" (إِنِّي آنَسْتُ ناراً), along with the emphasis and the use of the term "descry" (الإيناس), there is proof that he alone saw it, while the others with him did not. This is further supported by the initial phrase: "When he sighted a fire" (إِذْ رَأى ناراً). Likewise, His saying, "perhaps I may bring you..." (لَعَلِّي آتِيكُمْ) indicates an omission in the speech, the implied meaning being: "Wait so that I may go to it, perhaps I may bring you a brand from it or find at the fire a guide by whose guidance we may be led."

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا أَتاها نُودِيَ يا مُوسى إِنِّي أَنَا رَبُّكَ — "When he came to it, he was called, ‘O Moses! Indeed I am your Lord...’" (20:11-12) until His saying, "طُوىً" (Tuwa). Tuwa is the name of a valley at the Mount, which Allah, the Glorified, called "the holy valley" (الواد المقدس). This naming and description are the evidence that the command to remove his sandals was out of respect for the valley, so it would not be trodden upon with sandals. Furthermore, the fact that the removal of the sandals is branched (تفريع) from His saying, "Indeed I am your Lord," indicates that the holiness of the valley is due to it being a sanctuary of proximity and a place of presence and intimate discourse (المناجاة). Thus, the meaning of the verse resolves to something like: "He was called: O Moses, here I am, your Lord, and you are in My presence; the valley has become holy by this, so adhere to the requirements of etiquette and remove your sandals."

In this manner, certain places and times are sanctified, such as the Noble Kaaba, the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام), and other mosques and respected shrines (المشاهد) in Islam, as well as the Eids and blessed days. This holiness and nobility are acquired through association with a noble event that occurred therein or a sacred rite (نسك) and worship (عبادة) — the act of submission and devotion to Allah — legislated within them; otherwise, there is no inherent superiority between parts of space or parts of time.

When Moses (peace be upon him) heard the saying of the Exalted, "O Moses! Indeed I am your Lord," he understood with certain knowledge (فهم يقين) that the One speaking to him was his Lord and that the speech was His speech. This is because it was a revelation (وحي) from Him, the Exalted. Allah has explicitly stated: وَما كانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُكَلِّمَهُ اللهُ إِلَّا وَحْياً أَوْ مِنْ وَراءِ حِجابٍ أَوْ يُرْسِلَ رَسُولاً فَيُوحِيَ بِإِذْنِهِ ما يَشاءُ — "It is not for any human that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a veil, or by sending a messenger who reveals by His leave whatever He wishes" (42:51). This indicates that there is no intermediary between Him and the one He speaks to—neither a veil nor a messenger—when the speaking is by revelation (وحي). Since there was no assumed intermediary, the recipient of the revelation found no speaker for himself, nor could he imagine any, except Allah, and he found the speech to be none other than His speech. Had there been a possibility that the speaker was someone else or the speech belonged to another, it would not be a "speaking" where nothing exists between the human and his Lord.

This is the state of the Prophet and the Messenger at the very beginning of the revelation of prophethood (النبوة) and messengership (الرسالة); no doubt flickers within him, nor does any uncertainty cross his mind that the One revealing to him is Allah, the Glorified, without needing to employ discursive thought, seek evidence, or establish a proof. Had he required any of that, it would be an acquisition through the theoretical faculty (القوة النظرية), not a reception from the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — without an intermediary.

If you say: His saying, the Exalted, in the story in another place: وَنادَيْناهُ مِنْ جانِبِ الطُّورِ الْأَيْمَنِ وَقَرَّبْناهُ نَجِيًّا — "We called him from the right side of the Mount and drew him near for confidential discourse" (19:52), and His saying in another place: مِنْ شاطِئِ الْوادِ الْأَيْمَنِ فِي الْبُقْعَةِ الْمُبارَكَةِ مِنَ الشَّجَرَةِ — "...from the right bank of the valley in the blessed spot, from the tree" (28:30), establishes a "veil" (الحجاب) in His speaking to him (peace be upon him).

I say: Yes, but the existence of a veil or a messenger in the station of speaking does not negate the realization of speaking by revelation (وحي). For revelation, like all acts of the Exalted, is not devoid of an intermediary. The matter depends on the attention of the addressee who receives the speech. If he attends to the intermediary carrying the speech and is veiled by it from Him, the Exalted, then the speech is a "messengership" (رسالة) sent to him via an angel, for example, and a revelation from the angel. But if his attention is directed toward Him, the Exalted, then it is a revelation from Him, even if an intermediary exists to which he does not attend. Evidence for what we have mentioned is His saying in the following verse, addressing Moses: فَاسْتَمِعْ لِما يُوحى — "so listen to what is revealed" (20:13), wherein He called it "revelation" (وحي), despite having established the "veil" (الحجاب) in His other speech regarding it.

In short, His saying: "Indeed I am your Lord, so remove your sandals..." is an alerting of Moses to the fact that the situation is one of Presence and the station is one of direct address (المشافهة), and that He has secluded him and singled him out with increased care. For this reason, it was said: "Indeed I am your Lord," and He did not say: "I am Allah" or "I am the Lord of all the worlds." Also, for this reason, His second saying: "Indeed I am Allah" (إِنَّنِي أَنَا اللهُ) did not constitute a repetition, because the first was to clear the station of others for the delivery of revelation, and the second was part of the revelation itself.

In His saying: "he was called" (نُودِيَ), where the mention of the agent is omitted—He did not say: "We called him" or "Allah called him"—there is a subtlety beyond measure. It also hints that the appearance of this sign (الآية) to Moses occurred by way of surprise.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنَا اخْتَرْتُكَ فَاسْتَمِعْ لِما يُوحى — "I have chosen you, so listen to what is revealed" (20:13). "Selection" (الاختيار) is derived from khayr (good). Its reality is that the agent's affair fluctuates between several actions, and he must prefer one of them to perform; thus, he distinguishes which is the best (خيرها) and then acts upon its being better than others. His acting upon its being better than others is "selection." Therefore, selection is always for an end (غاية) which is the agent's purpose for his action.

Thus, His selection of Moses was for a divine end, which is the granting of prophethood and messengership. This is evidenced by His saying, as a branch of the selection: "so listen to what is revealed." The divine will (المشية الإلهية) was attached to sending a human to bear prophethood and messengership, and Moses was, in the knowledge of the Exalted, better than others and more fit for this purpose; so He chose him (peace be upon him).

His saying: "I have chosen you" (وَأَنَا اخْتَرْتُكَ), according to what the context provides, is of the nature of issuing the command for his prophethood and messengership; it is a performative (إنشاء), not an informative (إخبار) statement. Had it been informative, it would have been said: "And I have [already] chosen you." But it is the performative enactment of selection for prophethood and messengership through this very word. Then, once the selection was realized through His performative act, He branched from it the command to listen to the revelation containing his prophethood and messengership, saying: "so listen to what is revealed." Listening to what is revealed means paying close attention to it.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّنِي أَنَا اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاعْبُدْنِي وَأَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِذِكْرِي — "Indeed I am Allah—there is no god except Me. So worship Me, and maintain the prayer for My remembrance" (20:14). This is the revelation he (peace be upon him) was commanded to listen to, spanning eleven verses that encompass both prophethood and messengership. As for prophethood, it is in this verse and the two following it. As for messengership, it is taken from His saying: "What is that in your right hand, O Moses?" (20:17) and ends at His saying: "Go to Pharaoh, for he has rebelled" (20:24). Allah has explicitly stated that he was both a messenger and a prophet in His saying: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ مُوسى إِنَّهُ كانَ مُخْلَصاً وَكانَ رَسُولاً نَبِيًّا — "And mention Moses in the Book. Indeed he was dedicated, and he was a messenger and a prophet" (19:51).

In the three verses encompassing prophethood, both pillars are mentioned: the pillar of belief (الاعتقاد) and the pillar of action (العمل). The foundations of belief are three: monotheism (التوحيد), prophethood (النبوة), and the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and the final judgment. He mentioned monotheism and the Return and omitted prophethood because the speech was with the Prophet himself. As for the pillar of action, it was summarized—despite its details—in a single phrase: "So worship Me." Thus, the foundations of religion (أصول الدين) and its branches (فروعه) were completed in three verses.

His saying: "Indeed I am Allah—there is no god except Me" (إِنَّنِي أَنَا اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا) identified the One named by the Name through Himself, saying: "Indeed I am Allah," and He did not say: "Indeed Allah is I." This is because the requirement of Presence is that the description of a thing be identified by its essence, not its essence by its description. As the brothers of Joseph said when they recognized him: إِنَّكَ لَأَنْتَ يُوسُفُ قالَ أَنَا يُوسُفُ وَهذا أَخِي — "‘Are you indeed Joseph?’ He said, ‘I am Joseph, and this is my brother’" (12:90). Although the Name of Majesty (اسم الجلالة) is a proper noun for the Transcendent Essence, it conveys the meaning of the One named "Allah," for there is no path to the Sacred Essence itself. It is as if it were said: "I am the One who is called 'Allah'." The Speaker is present and witnessed, and the One named by the name "Allah" is as if it were ambiguous as to who He is; so it was said: "I am He." Furthermore, the Name of Majesty is a proper noun by predominance (علم بالغلبة) and is not devoid of a descriptive origin.

His saying: "there is no god except Me, so worship Me" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاعْبُدْنِي) — the word of monotheism is arranged upon His saying: "Indeed I am Allah" verbally, because it is arranged upon it in reality. For if He is the One from Whom everything begins, by Whom everything subsists, and to Whom everything returns, then it is not fitting that the submission of worship (العبادة) be shown to anyone but Him. He is the God worshipped in truth, there is no god besides Him. Therefore, He branched from that the command to worship Him, saying: "So worship Me."

His saying: "and maintain the prayer for My remembrance" (وَأَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِذِكْرِي) singled out prayer (الصلاة) for mention—which is an instance of mentioning the specific after the general to emphasize its importance—because prayer is the best action through which servile submission is represented, and through it, the remembrance (الذكر) of Allah, the Glorified, is realized just as the spirit is realized through its mold.

According to this meaning, His saying: "for My remembrance" (لِذِكْرِي) is the addition of the verbal noun to its object, and the lam is for causality (للتعليل), being connected to "maintain" (أقم). The sum of it is: "Realize your remembrance of Me through prayer," just as one says: "Eat to be full" or "Drink to be quenched." This is the meaning that first comes to mind from such a context.

There have been numerous opinions regarding His saying: "for My remembrance." It was said: it is connected to "maintain," as mentioned. It was said: to "prayer." It was said: to His saying: "So worship Me." Regarding the lam, it was said: it is for causality. It was said: for timing (للتوقيت), meaning: "Maintain the prayer at the time of My remembrance," or "at the time of remembering it if you forget it or it passes you by"; thus, it would be like the lam in His saying: أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِدُلُوكِ الشَّمْسِ — "Maintain the prayer from the sun's decline..." (17:78).

Regarding "remembrance" (الذكر), it was said: what is meant is the verbal remembrance contained within the prayer. It was said: the heart-centered remembrance that accompanies it and is realized through it, or which is arranged upon it and occurs through it as an effect from its cause, or the remembrance that precedes it. It was said: it refers to what is more general than both heart-centered and verbal remembrance.

Regarding the genitive construction (الإضافة), it was said: it is the addition of the verbal noun to its object. It was said: it is the addition of the verbal noun to its agent, meaning: "Pray so that I may remember you with praise and reward," or "Pray for My mention of it in the Heavenly Books and My command for it."

It was said: it implies the restriction (قصر) of the maintenance to the remembrance, meaning: "Maintain the prayer for the purpose of My remembrance, not for any other purpose such as a reward you hope for or a punishment you fear." It was said: there is no such restriction.

It was said: it implies the restriction of the added word to the word it is added to, meaning: "Maintain the prayer for My remembrance specifically, without showing off or mixing it with the remembrance of others." It was said: there is no indication of this from the perspective of the wording, even if it is true in itself.

It was said: what is meant by "remembrance" is the remembrance of the prayer itself, meaning: "Maintain the prayer upon remembering it or for the sake of remembering it." The speech would then be based on an omitted word, the original being: "for the remembrance of my prayer," or on the basis that the remembrance of prayer is a cause for the remembrance of Allah, so the effect was mentioned while the cause was intended—among other views. The resulting opinions range from the weak to the substantial. What is first understood is what we have presented.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ السَّاعَةَ آتِيَةٌ أَكادُ أُخْفِيها لِتُجْزى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما تَسْعى — "Indeed the Hour is coming. I almost keep it hidden, so that every soul may be rewarded for what it strives" (20:15). This is a justification for His saying in the previous verse: "So worship Me." This does not contradict the fact that "So worship Me" was branched from the word of monotheism mentioned before it. For although the obligation of worshipping Him, the Exalted, is branched from His oneness in itself, it would have no effect were it not for the establishment of a Day in which man is rewarded for what he has done, and in which the doer of good is distinguished from the doer of evil, and the obedient from the disobedient. Otherwise, legislation would be futile and command and prohibition would be in vain, having no effect. Therefore, it was a decree of certain judgment, and the denial of doubt regarding it is repeated in His speech.

His saying: "I almost keep it hidden" (أَكادُ أُخْفِيها) — the apparent meaning of the absolute use of "hiding" is that it means: "I am close to hiding it and concealing it so that I do not inform about it at all, so that its occurrence may be more profound in its suddenness and more intense in its surprise." It shall not come except suddenly, as He, the Exalted, said: لا تَأْتِيكُمْ إِلَّا بَغْتَةً — "It will not come to you but suddenly" (7:187). Or: "I am close to not informing about it so that the sincere may be distinguished from others." For most people only worship Him, the Exalted, out of hope for His reward or fear of His punishment as a recompense for obedience and disobedience. The truest action is that which is for the sake of Allah, not out of greed for Paradise or fear of Hell. If the Day of Recompense were hidden and concealed, then those who perform the reality of worship would be distinguished from others.

It was said: the meaning of "I almost keep it hidden" is: "I am close to concealing it from Myself," which is an exaggeration in concealment. When someone wishes to exaggerate the concealment of something, he says: "I almost hid it from myself," meaning: "How then would I disclose it to others?" This has been attributed to a narration.

His saying: "so that every soul may be rewarded for what it strives" (لِتُجْزى كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما تَسْعى) is connected to His saying: "is coming" (آتِيَةٌ), and the meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلا يَصُدَّنَّكَ عَنْها مَنْ لا يُؤْمِنُ بِها وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ فَتَرْدى — "So do not let him who does not believe in it and follows his desires bar you from it, lest you should perish" (20:16).
