It is possible he was a materialist (دهرياً) denying the Maker (الصانع) entirely, perhaps claiming that the contingent (الممكن) does not need an efficient cause (مؤثر) and that the existence of the world is accidental, as related from Democritus and his followers.

It is also possible he was a philosopher affirming a necessitating cause (العلة الموجبة), or that he was among the worshippers of the stars, or the worshippers of idols, or from the incarnationists (الحلولية) and anthropomorphists (المجسمة). As for his claim of lordship (الربوبية) for himself, it meant that those under his hand must obey him, submit to him, and not occupy themselves with the obedience of another." (End of summary).

However, by returning to the core of their doctrine, you will realize that none of these statements or possibilities, nor what they argued with, accords with the reality of the matter.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ رَبُّنَا الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى "He said, ‘Our Lord is He who gave everything its creation and then guided it.’" (20:50). The context of the verse—occurring as a response to Pharaoh’s question: "Who is your Lord, O Moses?"—indicates that "its creation" (خَلْقَهُ) is used as a verbal noun (اسم المصدر) and the pronoun refers to "the thing" (الشيء); thus, what is meant is the specific existence (الوجود الخاص) of the thing.

Guidance (الهداية) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — is either showing the thing the path leading to its objective or bringing it to its objective. These two meanings actually return to a single concept: a type of bringing the thing to its objective, either by bringing it to the objective itself or to the path leading to it. Guidance is mentioned here without specifying the guided or the destination. Since nothing preceded in the speech except "the thing" which was given its creation, it appears that what is meant is the guidance of "everything"—mentioned before—to its objective. Its objective is the end (الغاية) with which its existence is linked and at which it terminates. This objective is its goal by virtue of the "creation" it was given. The meaning of His guiding it to that goal is His directing it toward it, all of this through the suitability of one part to another.

The meaning thus results in His establishing the link between every thing—through the powers and instruments it is equipped with in its existence—and its effects which lead it to the end of its existence. For example, the human embryo, which is a zygote (نطفة) formed in the human image, is equipped within itself with powers and organs suitable for the actions and effects that lead it to becoming a complete human in soul and body. The human zygote, with its inherent potential, was given its specific "creation"—which is the specific human existence—and then guided and directed, through the powers and organs it was equipped with, toward its objective, which is the end of human existence and the final perfection (الكمال الأخير) specific to this species.

From here, the meaning of conjoining His saying "guided" (هَدى) to His saying "gave everything its creation" (أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ) with the particle "then" (ثُمَّ) becomes clear. What is meant is a posteriority in rank (التأخر الرتبي), for the movement and course of a thing after its existence is posterior in rank. In physical beings (الموجودات الجسمانية), this posteriority is gradual and temporal (زماني) in a sense.

It also becomes clear that what is meant by guidance is the general guidance (الهداية العامة) encompassing everything, rather than the specific guidance for humans. This is understood by analyzing specific guidance and generalizing it by stripping away specificities. The reality of human guidance is showing him the path leading to the objective, and the path is the link between the seeker and his objective. Thus, everything equipped with that which links it to something and moves it toward it has been "guided" to that thing. Every thing is guided toward its perfection through the equipment it has been provided with, and Allah, the Glorified, is the Guide (الهادي).

Thus, the system of action and passion (الفعل والانفعال) in things—or if you prefer, the particular system (النظام الجزئي) specific to each thing and the general system (النظام العام) encompassing all particular systems in terms of the connection of their parts and the transition of things from one part to another—is a manifestation of His guidance. From another perspective, it is a manifestation of His governance (تدبيره). It is known that governance terminates at creation (الخلق); meaning, the one to whom the governance of things is attributed and terminates is the one who brought the things themselves into existence. Every existence or attribute of existence terminates in Him and subsists through Him.

It has thus been clarified that the speech—namely His saying: "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it"—contains the proof (البرهان) for His being the Lord of everything and that there is no lord besides Him. For His creating things and bringing them into existence necessitates His ownership of their existences—since they subsist through Him—and His ownership of the governance of their affairs.

At this point, it becomes evident that the speech follows its natural order and the context proceeds according to the requirements of the situation. The situation is one of calling to monotheism and obedience to the Messenger. Pharaoh, after hearing the call, responded by feigning ignorance of Allah being his Lord, and interpreted their speech as a call to their own Lord, asking: "Who is your Lord?" It was appropriate to respond that "Our Lord is the Lord of all the worlds" (رب العالمين) to include them, him, and everyone else. However, the response given was more eloquent than that: "Our Lord is He who gave everything its creation and then guided it." Thus, the response was that He is the Lord of everything, and it simultaneously provided the proof for this claim. Had it been said, "Our Lord is the Lord of all the worlds," it would have provided the claim only, without the proof. Understand this.

Guidance (الهداية) was affirmed in the speech rather than governance (التدبير), even though their application is the same as previously indicated, because the context is one of calling (الدعوة) and guidance, and general guidance is more suitable for it.

This is what reflection upon the holy verse leads to. Through this, the status of other interpretations offered for the verse is known:

Such as the view of some that "its creation" (خَلْقَهُ) means "the like of its creation," which is the mate (الزوج) that resembles the thing; thus the meaning would be: "He who created for everything a mate," being similar in meaning to His saying: "And of everything We created pairs."

And the view of some that "everything" refers to types of blessings (النعم)—acting as the second object of "gave"—and "creation" refers to the created being (المخلوق)—acting as the first object. The meaning would be: "He who gave His creatures every kind of blessing."

And the view of some that guidance means instruction and pointing toward the existence of Allah the Exalted and His oneness without partner. The meaning would be: "He who gave everything the existence it seeks by the tongue of its potential, then instructed and pointed through that to the existence of His self and His oneness."

Contemplation of what has passed is sufficient for you to realize the invalidity of these views; they are meanings far removed from the context and are restrictions of the verse's wording without a restrictor.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ فَما بالُ الْقُرُونِ الْأُولى "He said, ‘What about the former generations?’" (20:51). It is said that "the state" (البال) originally means thought (الفكر), from which comes the saying "such occurred to my mind" (خطر ببالي). Then it was used to mean "condition" (الحال). It is not dualized or pluralized; the usage balat is anomalous.

Since the response of Moses (peace be upon him) included the concept of general guidance—which in humans is not complete except through prophethood and the Return (المعاد), as the religion of monotheism is not upright except with an accounting and a reward by which the doer of good is distinguished from the doer of evil, and that is not complete except by distinguishing what Allah commands from what He forbids, and what He is pleased with from what He dislikes—and since the message they were commanded to deliver to Pharaoh explicitly included the reward, as stated at its end: إِنَّا قَدْ أُوحِيَ إِلَيْنا أَنَّ الْعَذابَ عَلى مَنْ كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّى "Indeed it has been revealed to us that the punishment will be for him who denies and turns away" (20:48), and since the idolaters denied this, Pharaoh turned away from discussing lordship (الربوبية)—having been silenced by Moses' response—to the matter of the Return, asking about it based on its perceived impossibility.

Thus, his saying: "What about the former generations?" (فَما بالُ الْقُرُونِ الْأُولى) means: what is the condition of the past nations and human generations who died and perished, leaving no news or trace? How will they be rewarded for their deeds when there is no doer in existence and no deed, and they are today nothing but stories and legends? The verse is similar to what is related from the polytheists in His saying: وَقالُوا أَإِذا ضَلَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ "They say, ‘When we have been lost in the earth, shall we indeed be [recreated] in a new creation?’" (32:10). The apparent meaning of the speech is that it is based on the perceived impossibility due to the absence of knowledge about them and their deeds because of death and passing, as evidenced by the response of Moses (peace be upon him).

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ عِلْمُها عِنْدَ رَبِّي فِي كِتابٍ لا يَضِلُّ رَبِّي وَلا يَنْسى "He said, ‘The knowledge of them is with my Lord in a Book. My Lord neither errs nor forgets.’" (20:52). He (peace be upon him) answered his question by affirming the absolute knowledge of Allah regarding the details of those past generations. He said: "The knowledge of them is with my Lord" (عِلْمُها عِنْدَ رَبِّي), using "knowledge" in an absolute sense; thus, nothing of their persons or deeds escapes Him. He placed this knowledge "with Allah" (عِنْدَ اللهِ) so it is never absent from Him nor lost, and Allah the Exalted has said: وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ باقٍ "...and what is with Allah is enduring" (16:96). Then he restricted that by saying: "in a Book" (فِي كِتابٍ)—which is perhaps a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the knowledge—to emphasize that it is recorded and preserved without changing from its state. The "Book" is mentioned in the indefinite to indicate the magnitude of its affair in terms of its vast encompassment and precision, for it leaves out neither small nor large but accounts for it.

The meaning of the speech thus results in: the reward of the former generations would only be problematic if they were unknown and not recognized, but they are known to my Lord, preserved with Him in a Book that is not subject to error, change, absence, or cessation.

His saying: "My Lord neither errs nor forgets" (لا يَضِلُّ رَبِّي وَلا يَنْسى) is a negation of initial ignorance and ignorance after knowledge, according to what is related from some. However, it appears that the sentence is intended to negate both types of ignorance after knowledge. For "erring" (الضلال) is seeking a goal by following a path that does not lead to it but to something else; thus, error in knowledge is taking a thing in place of another, which only occurs by the known object—insofar as it is known—changing from what it was in the knowledge initially. "Forgetting" (النسيان) is the exit of a thing from knowledge after it had entered it. Both are forms of ignorance after knowledge, and their negation is suitable for the initial affirmation of knowledge. Thus, the sum of the verse indicates that He is knowledgeable of the former generations, and there is no way for Him to be ignorant after knowing; therefore, He will reward them based on what He knows.

From here, it appears that His saying: "My Lord neither errs nor forgets" is part of the complete explanation of the verse, as if it were a rebuttal to a hypothetical objection: "Even if they were known one day, today their essences are void and their traces are effaced, such that nothing can be distinguished from another." The response was that nothing of them, their traces, or their deeds is confused for Him through the change of error, nor is it absent from Him through forgetting. For this reason, the sentence was brought as a separate statement without a conjunction.

Knowledge was affirmed and ignorance negated for Allah the Exalted under the title of "Lord" (رب) so that it might contain an indication of the proof for the claim. This is because the premise of lordship (الربوبية) is incompatible with the premise of ignorance of the subject of lordship (المربوب). The premise of His absolute lordship over everything—and the Lord is the owner of the thing and the governor of its affairs—necessitates that things are owned by Him, their existence subsisting through Him from every aspect, and that they are governed by Him. However they are conceived, they are known to Him. If any of them were assumed to be unknown to Him through error, forgetting, or initial ignorance, then that thing—whatever it may be and wherever it is realized—is owned by Him, its existence subsisting through Him, and governed by His governance. There is no veil or separator between them; this is the presence (الحضور) which we call knowledge. To assume it is unknown—meaning absent from Him—is a contradiction.

He attributed "Lord" to himself in the verse in two places. The second is the placement of a manifest noun in place of a pronoun, as has been said. He did not say "Our Lord" (رَبُّنَا) as in the previous verse because the previous question was about the Lord of both of them to whom they were calling; thus, the response matched it. Its meaning according to the context was: "The Lord to whom I and my brother call is such and such." As for this verse, the question was about a matter pertaining to the former generations, and the one describing it is Moses; thus, the meaning was: "The Lord whom I describe is knowledgeable of them." That which conveys this meaning is "my Lord" (رَبِّي) and nothing else. Reflect on this, for it is subtle.

The point in the second "my Lord" is similar to that in the first. As for it being a case of placing a manifest noun in place of a pronoun, there is some doubt due to the sentence being separate.

The statements of the exegetes have differed greatly in interpreting these two verses with various views and possibilities, which we have refrained from mentioning due to their lack of benefit. Among the most strange is the view of many that Pharaoh’s saying to Moses: "What about the former generations?" was a question about the history of the ancient, vanished nations. He asked Moses this to distract him from discussing the principles of divine knowledge (المعارف الإلهية) and establishing the proof for the manifest Truth regarding the matters of the Beginning and the Return—which the idolaters deny—and to occupy him with the useless history of the ancients and news of the past. Moses' response: "The knowledge of them is with my Lord..." etc., would then mean returning the knowledge of them to Allah and that it is from the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — which none knows but the Knower of the Unseen.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ مَهْداً "He who made the earth a cradle for you..." until His saying: لَآياتٍ لِأُولِي النُّهى "...are signs for those who possess intellect" (20:53-54). You have already recognized that his question "What about the former generations?" is linked to what Allah was described with regarding general guidance, part of which is the guidance of man to his happiness in life, which is the eternal afterlife. Similarly, the response to it: "The knowledge of them is with my Lord..." etc., is linked. Thus, His saying: "He who made the earth a cradle for you" continues the discourse on general guidance and mentions prominent witnesses of that.

Allah, the Glorified, settled man on the earth to live an earthly life therein, so that he might take from it provisions for his high, heavenly life, just as a child is settled in a cradle (المهد) and raised for a life that is more noble and higher than it. He made paths (سبلاً) for man therein so that he might realize thereby that between him and his goal—which is proximity to Him, the Exalted, and entering the enclosure of honor—is a path that must be traversed, just as earthly paths are traversed for his vital needs. He sent down water from the sky—which is rainwater, and from it come the springs of the earth, its rivers, and its seas—and brought forth thereby pairs (أزواجاً), meaning various and diverse closely related types and species of plants, guiding you to eat them. In that are signs (آيات) that point those of intellect to His guidance and lordship.

Thus, His saying: "He who made the earth a cradle for you" is an indication of man’s settlement on the earth for the continuation of life, which is part of guidance. His saying: "and threaded for you paths therein" (وَسَلَكَ لَكُمْ فِيها سُبُلاً) is an indication of the routes man traverses on earth to achieve his objectives, which is also part of guidance. His saying: "and sent down water from the sky, and by it We brought forth pairs of various plants. Eat and pasture your cattle" (وَأَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَخْرَجْنا بِهِ أَزْواجاً مِنْ نَباتٍ شَتَّى كُلُوا وَارْعَوْا أَنْعامَكُمْ) is an indication of the guidance of man and cattle to eat plants for the preservation of life. In it is also the guidance of the sky to rain, the rainwater to descend, and the plants to emerge.

The preposition bi in "by it" (بِهِ) denotes causality (السببية), and in it is an affirmation of causality and effectuality (المسببية) among cosmic affairs. What is meant by the plants being "pairs" (أزواجاً) is their being closely related types and species, as interpreted by the scholars, or the reality of pairing between male and female in plants, which is one of the realities pointed out by the Mighty Book.

In His saying: "and by it We brought forth pairs of various plants" (فأخرجنا به أزواجاً من نبات شتى), there is a transition (التفات) from the third person to the first person plural. It is said that the reason for this is that this wondrous creation and the origination of diverse forms and different pairs—despite the variety of life within them—from a single water, is of such greatness and magnificent craftsmanship that it only issues from the Great. The great speak of themselves and others among their helpers. This transition has occurred regarding the bringing forth of plants by water in several places in His speech, such as His saying: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَخْرَجْنا بِهِ ثَمَراتٍ مُخْتَلِفاً أَلْوانُها "Have you not regarded that Allah sends down water from the sky, with which We bring forth fruits of varying colours?" (35:27), and His saying: وَأَنْزَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَنْبَتْنا بِهِ حَدائِقَ ذاتَ بَهْجَةٍ "...and sent down for you water from the sky, whereby We caused to grow delightful gardens" (27:60), and His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَخْرَجْنا بِهِ نَباتَ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ "It is He who sends down water from the sky, and with it We bring forth the growth of every kind" (6:99).

His saying: "Indeed in that are signs for those who possess intellect" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِأُولِي النُّهى). Al-Nuha (النهى) is the plural of nuhyah, which is the intellect (العقل); it is so named because it forbids (تنهى) one from following whims.

His saying, the Exalted: مِنْها خَلَقْناكُمْ وَفِيها نُعِيدُكُمْ وَمِنْها نُخْرِجُكُمْ تارَةً أُخْرى "From it We created you, into it We will return you, and from it We will bring you forth another time." (20:55). The pronoun refers to the earth. The verse describes the beginning of man’s creation from the earth, then his return into it to become part of it, then his being brought forth from it to return to Allah. Thus, it contains the complete cycle of man’s guidance.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَرَيْناهُ آياتِنا كُلَّها فَكَذَّبَ وَأَبى "Certainly We showed him all Our signs, but he denied and refused." (20:56). It appears that what is meant by the signs are the staff, the hand, and the other signs that Moses showed Pharaoh during the days of his call before the drowning, as passed in His saying: "Go, you and your brother, with My signs." Thus, what is meant is all the signs he was shown, even if they were not all brought at the beginning of the call, just as what is meant by "he denied and refused" is his absolute denial and refusal, not just what he manifested of them at the start.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ أَجِئْتَنا لِتُخْرِجَنا مِنْ أَرْضِنا بِسِحْرِكَ يا مُوسى "He said, ‘Have you come to us to expel us from our land with your magic, O Moses?’" (20:57). The pronoun refers to Pharaoh. He accused Moses, first, of magic (السحر) so that he would not be obliged to recognize the truth of the miraculous signs he brought and the reality of his call. Second, he accused him of wanting to expel the Copts from their land, which is the land of Egypt. This is a political accusation by which he intended to turn the people away from him and incite their thoughts against him as an enemy who wants to drive them out of their environment and homeland through his trickery; and there is no life for one who has no environment.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَنَأْتِيَنَّكَ بِسِحْرٍ مِثْلِهِ فَاجْعَلْ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكَ مَوْعِداً لا نُخْلِفُهُ نَحْنُ وَلا أَنْتَ مَكاناً سُوىً "We will surely bring you magic like it. So make an appointment between us and you, which neither we nor you shall fail, at a place midway." (20:58). It appears, as the following verse witnesses, that "appointment" (الموعد) is a noun of time. "Failing the appointment" (إخلاف الوعد) is the failure to act according to its requirement. "A place midway" (مكاناً سُوىً)—with a damma on the sin—means located at the midpoint of the distance, or with level sides without elevation or depression. It is said in al-Mufradat: "A place suwan and sawa’un is the middle. It is said sawa’un, suwan, and siwan—with damma or kasra on the sin—meaning its two sides are equal. It is used as an adjective and an adverb, and its origin is a verbal noun." (End of quote).

The meaning is: "I swear we shall surely bring you magic that matches your magic to sever your argument and nullify your intent. So set between us and you a time of appointment that we shall not fail, in a place between us, or in a place with level sides, or set between us and you such a place."

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ مَوْعِدُكُمْ يَوْمُ الزِّينَةِ وَأَنْ يُحْشَرَ النَّاسُ ضُحًى "He said, ‘Your appointment shall be the day of the festival, and let the people be gathered in the forenoon.’" (20:59). The pronoun refers to Moses. He set the appointment for the "day of the festival" (يوم الزينة). It appears from the context that it was a day for them that served as an Eid. It appears from the wording that they used to adorn themselves on that day and decorate the markets. "Gathering the people" (حشر الناس)—according to what al-Raghib mentioned—is bringing them out of their dwellings and urging them toward war or the like. The "forenoon" (الضحى) is the time when the sun spreads during the day.

His saying: "and let the people be gathered in the forenoon" (وَأَنْ يُحْشَرَ النَّاسُ ضُحًى) is conjoined to "the festival" or to "day" with "day" or "time" being implied. The meaning is: Moses said, "Your appointment is the day of the festival and the day of the gathering of the people in the forenoon." It is not unlikely that it is an object of accompaniment (مفعول معه), meaning: "Your appointment is the day of the festival along with the gathering of the people in the forenoon," which amounts to a condition. He stipulated this so that what he brings and what they bring would be before the eyes of the people at a visible hour.

His saying, the Exalted: فَتَوَلَّى فِرْعَوْنُ فَجَمَعَ كَيْدَهُ ثُمَّ أَتى "So Pharaoh withdrew and mustered his stratagem, and then he came." (20:60). The apparent context is that Pharaoh’s "withdrawing" (تولي) refers to his departure from the assembly of the appointment to prepare for what was promised. "Mustering his stratagem" (جمع كيده) means gathering the means of his plot, namely the magicians and other things used to blind the people and deceive them. It is possible that "mustering his stratagem" means gathering those who possess stratagem, with the possessed noun being omitted, referring to the magicians and his other workers and helpers. His saying: "then he came" (ثُمَّ أَتى) means he then came to the appointment and attended it.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ لَهُمْ مُوسى وَيْلَكُمْ لا تَفْتَرُوا عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً فَيُسْحِتَكُمْ بِعَذابٍ وَقَدْ خابَ مَنِ افْتَرى "Moses said to them, ‘Woe to you! Do not fabricate a lie against Allah, or He will uproot you with a punishment. Whoever fabricates [a lie] will certainly fail.’" (20:61). Al-Wayl (الويل) is a word of punishment and threat; its origin means torment. The meaning of "woe to you" is "may Allah punish you with a torment." Al-Saht (السحت)—with a fatha on the sin—is the uprooting of hair by shaving, and al-ishat (الإسحات) is uprooting and destruction.

In His saying: "Moses said to them, ‘Woe to you! Do not fabricate a lie against Allah...’", the plural pronouns in the third and second person refer to Pharaoh and his "stratagem," who are the magicians and his other helpers against Moses (peace be upon him), as they were mentioned in the previous verse. As for them referring to the magicians only, they were not mentioned previously, nor does any linguistic evidence point to them.

This statement from Moses (peace be upon him) is an exhortation to them and a warning against fabricating a lie against Allah. Their fabrication, as mentioned previously, included Pharaoh calling the divine signs "magic" and casting the true call as a means to expel them from their land. Magic is also a fabrication, but "fabricating a lie against Allah"—which is inventing a lie against Him—only occurs by attributing to Allah what is not from Him. Counting the miraculous sign as magic and the true call as a political plot severed their attribution to Allah. Similarly, their bringing magic against the miracle while admitting it was magic with no reality does not count as fabricating against Allah.

Thus, it appears that what is meant by "fabricating a lie against Allah" is believing in the principles of idolatry, such as the divinity of the gods, their intercession, and the return of the world's governance to them, as some have interpreted the verse. This has been counted as fabrication against Allah in several places in the Quran, such as His saying: قَدِ افْتَرَيْنا عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً إِنْ عُدْنا فِي مِلَّتِكُمْ "We would have fabricated a lie against Allah if we were to return to your creed..." (7:89).

His saying: "or He will uproot you with a punishment" (فَيُسْحِتَكُمْ بِعَذابٍ) is a result of the prohibition; meaning: "Do not associate partners with Allah, lest He uproot you and destroy you with a punishment because of your polytheism." The indefiniteness of "punishment" indicates its severity and magnitude.

His saying: "Whoever fabricates [a lie] will certainly fail" (وَقَدْ خابَ مَنِ افْتَرى). Al-Khaybah (الخيبة) is the despair of reaching the hoped-for result. This sentence is placed in the speech as a universal principle to be held onto, and so it is. For fabrication is a form of lying, and its causality is a false causality. False causes do not lead to true effects or sincere traces; their results are not fit for endurance, nor do they lead to happiness. There is nothing in their end but ill-omen and loss. Thus, the verse is more encompassing in meaning than His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللهِ الْكَذِبَ لا يُفْلِحُونَ "Indeed those who fabricate lies against Allah will not be felicitous" (10:69), because it affirms failure for fabrication in an absolute sense, unlike the second verse. A discussion on how lying does not lead to felicity has passed under the commentary of His saying: وَجاؤُ عَلى قَمِيصِهِ بِدَمٍ كَذِبٍ "And they brought his shirt with false blood on it" (12:18) in the eleventh volume of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: فَتَنازَعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ وَأَسَرُّوا النَّجْوى "So they disputed their matter among themselves and kept their counsel secret." (20:62) until His saying: مَنِ اسْتَعْلى "...whoever gains the upper hand" (20:64). "Disputing" (التنازع) is close in meaning to "differing" (الاختلاف), from naz' meaning to pull a thing from its place to uproot it. Tanazu' can be transitive by itself, as in the verse, or with the preposition fi, as in His saying: فَإِنْ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ "Then if you dispute concerning anything..." (4:59).

Al-Najwa (النجوى) is the speech that is whispered; its origin is a verbal noun meaning "confidential talk" (المناجاة), which is whispering in speech. Al-Muthla (المثلى) is the feminine of amthal, like fudla and afdal, meaning the closest or most similar. "The excellent tradition" (الطريقة المثلى) is the way that is closest to the truth or to their desire, which is the tradition of idolatry by which Egypt was then governed—the worship of the gods, at the head of whom was Pharaoh, the god of the Copts. "Mustering" (الإجماع)—according to what al-Raghib mentioned—is gathering a thing through thought and deliberation. "Rank" (الصف) is placing things on a straight line, like humans or trees. It is used as a verbal noun and a noun of the verbal noun. His saying: "then come in a rank" (ثُمَّ ائْتُوا صَفًّا) could be a verbal noun, or it could mean "in ranks," meaning: "Come to it with unity and agreement without differing or separating, lest you weaken; be as one hand against him."

It appears from the result of His saying: "So they disputed their matter" (فَتَنازَعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ) following what was in the previous verse: "Moses said to them..." etc., that the dispute and difference only appeared among them due to the exhortation Moses gave them. It had some effect on them, as is the nature of such words, for it was nothing but a word of truth with no ambiguity. Its summary was: "You have no knowledge of what you claim regarding the divinity of the gods and their intercession; thus, your attributing partners and intercessors to Allah is a fabrication against Him, and whoever fabricates will fail." This is a clear proof with no veil or dust upon it.

It appears from the subsequent saying related from the magicians: إِنَّا آمَنَّا بِرَبِّنا لِيَغْفِرَ لَنا خَطايانا وَما أَكْرَهْتَنا عَلَيْهِ مِنَ السِّحْرِ "Indeed we have believed in our Lord that He may forgive us our sins and the magic you compelled us to [perform]" (20:73), that the difference first appeared among the magicians. Perhaps His subsequent saying: "then come in a rank" (ثُمَّ ائْتُوا صَفًّا) suggests that those who were hesitant to confront Moses, or those determined to abandon the confrontation entirely, were some of the magicians, if the address was directed to them. Perhaps the context supports this.

In any case, when Pharaoh and his aides saw the dispute of the people—which contained their disgrace and abandonment—they kept their counsel secret (أَسَرُّوا النَّجْوى). they did not speak to them regarding the wisdom and exhortation Moses had delivered; rather, they turned away from that to what Pharaoh had accused him of—magic—and proposed a political plan to expel the Coptic nation from their land. The nation would not accept this, for it meant leaving their homes and wealth and falling from the peak of their happiness to the abyss of misery, especially as they saw what the Children of Israel were enduring among them.

They added to this another matter, more serious than exile and leaving homes and wealth: the disappearance of their "excellent tradition" (طريقتهم المثلى) and their national custom, which was the idolatrous creed that had ruled them century after century and generation after generation. Their bones had strengthened by it and their flesh had grown upon it. The common folk sanctify national traditions, especially those they have become accustomed to and believe to be manifest heavenly traditions. This was, in reality, an incitement for them to remain firm and steadfast in the idolatrous creed—not because it was a true religion with no doubt, for Moses' argument had clarified its corruption and exposed its falsehood—but under the title of it being a sacred national tradition upon which their nationality relied, their power and greatness were based, and their life was protected. If they differed and abandoned the confrontation with Moses, and he gained the upper hand over them, it would mean their total annihilation.

Thus, the opinion was that they should muster every stratagem they had, then abandon the dispute and come in a rank so that they might gain the upper hand; and "whoever gains the upper hand today will be felicitous."

They emphasized this to them through deceptive persuasion to unite and agree, and not to weaken in preserving their creed and civilization, and to charge at their enemy as one man. Pharaoh accompanied this with beautiful promises he made to them, as appears from His saying in another place: قالُوا لِفِرْعَوْنَ أَإِنَّ لَنا لَأَجْراً إِنْ كُنَّا نَحْنُ الْغالِبِينَ ، قالَ نَعَمْ وَإِنَّكُمْ إِذاً لَمِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ "They said to Pharaoh, ‘Shall we have a reward if we are the victors?’ He said, ‘Yes, and you will then indeed be among those stationed near [me].’" (26:41-42). By whatever means of enticement or intimidation, they compelled them to stand firm and face Moses in competition.

This is what reflection on the meaning of the verses provides, aided by the context, connected clues, and separate witnesses. Accordingly, His saying: "So they disputed their matter among themselves" (فَتَنازَعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ) refers to their difference following Moses' exhortation and the proof he alluded to.

His saying: "and kept their counsel secret" (وَأَسَرُّوا النَّجْوى) refers to their whispering regarding Moses' affair and their effort to remove the difference arising from hearing Moses' sermon (peace be upon him). His saying: "They said, ‘These two are indeed magicians who want...’" etc., is an explanation of the counsel they kept secret among themselves, the meaning of which has been clarified.

Regarding His saying: إِنْ هذانِ لَساحِرانِ "These two are indeed magicians" (20:63), the well-known reading is in (إِنْ) with a kasra on the hamza and a sukun on the nun. It is the particle inna (إِنَّ) that resembles a verb, lightened (مخففة) and thus rendered non-functional in terms of nasb of the noun and raf' of the predicate.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا يا مُوسى إِمَّا أَنْ تُلْقِيَ وَإِمَّا أَنْ نَكُونَ أَوَّلَ مَنْ أَلْقى "They said, ‘O Moses! Either you throw, or we will be the first to throw.’" (20:65) until the end of the following verse. "Ropes" (الحبال) is the plural of habl, and "staffs" (العصي) is the plural of asa. The magicians had used them to depict, in the eyes of the people, snakes and serpents like what appeared from the staff of Moses (peace be upon him).

Here there is an omission and brevity, as if it were said: "They came to the appointment and Moses was present. It was asked: What did they do? It was said: They said, ‘O Moses! Either you throw—meaning your staff—or we will be the first to throw.’" This was a choice they gave Moses between starting the throwing or waiting until they threw and then bringing what he would bring. "Moses said, ‘Rather, you throw.’" (قالَ موسى : بَلْ أَلْقُوا). He cleared the field for them so they could bring what they would bring, while he was reliant on his Lord, trusting in His promise without anxiety or agitation. His Lord had said to him: إِنَّنِي مَعَكُما أَسْمَعُ وَأَرى "Indeed I am with you both, hearing and seeing" (20:46).

His saying: "and lo! their ropes and their staffs seemed to him, due to their magic, to be sliding" (فَإِذا حِبالُهُمْ وَعِصِيُّهُمْ يُخَيَّلُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ سِحْرِهِمْ أَنَّها تَسْعى). There is an omission here; the estimation is: "So they threw, and lo! their ropes and staffs..." etc. The omission is to emphasize the suddenness, as if he (peace be upon him), when he said to them "Rather, you throw," did not wait before witnessing what he witnessed, without their throwing of the ropes and staffs being mentioned in between.

That which was imagined by Moses was also imagined by the other onlookers among the people, as He mentioned in another place: سَحَرُوا أَعْيُنَ النَّاسِ وَاسْتَرْهَبُوهُمْ "...they bewitched the people’s eyes and overawed them" (7:116). However, He mentioned Moses here among them to serve as a prelude to what is in the following verse.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَوْجَسَ فِي نَفْسِهِ خِيفَةً مُوسى "So Moses felt a fear within himself." (20:67). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "Al-Wajs (الوجس) is the hidden sound; al-tawajjus (التوجس) is listening; and al-iujas (الإيجاس) is the presence of that in the soul." He said: "So he felt a fear from them" (فَأَوْجَسَ مِنْهُمْ خِيفَةً). Thus, the wajs is a state that occurs in the soul after the hajas (الهاجس), because the hajas is the beginning of thinking, then the wajis is the passing thought (الخاطر). (End of quote).

Thus, "feeling fear within the soul" is sensing it therein. It is only light and hidden, its effect not appearing on the surface of the skin. Its existence in the soul is followed by the appearance of an ill thought therein, without an endorsement of what it necessitates of caution and wariness; otherwise, its effect would certainly appear on the surface of the skin and in the person's actions. The indefiniteness of "fear" (خيفة) points to this, as if it were said: "He sensed within himself a type of fear that is not given weight." It is strange that some said the indefiniteness is for magnification (التفخيم) and that the fear was great; this is an error. Had it been so, its effect would have appeared on the surface of his skin, and there would have been no reason to restrict the fear to being "within himself."

It has thus become clear that the fear he felt within himself was a momentary sensation, similar to the passing thought that followed it. The greatness of their magic occurred to his heart—that according to the imagination, it was similar to or near his sign—so he felt fear from this occurrence. It is like the occurrence itself: it has no lasting effect.

It is said: He feared that the matter would become confused for the people, such that they would not distinguish between his sign and their magic due to the similarity, thus doubting, not believing, and not following him. He did not yet know that his staff would swallow what they had faked.

However, this contradicts his tranquility in Allah and his trust in His command, for his Lord had said to him before that: بِآياتِنا أَنْتُما وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَكُمَا الْغالِبُونَ "...with Our signs, you two and those who follow you shall be the victors" (28:35).

It is also said: He feared that the people would disperse after seeing their magic and not wait until he threw his staff, thus claiming equality and causing the effort to fail.

However, this is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse. The apparent result of His saying: "So Moses felt a fear within himself..." following His saying: "it seemed to him, due to their magic..." is that he feared what was imagined to him from their magic, not that he feared the dispersal of the people before the matter was clarified by throwing the staff. Had he feared that, he would not have allowed them to throw their ropes and staffs first. Furthermore, this view does not suit the saying of Allah the Exalted in strengthening his soul: "We said, ‘Do not be afraid. Indeed you will have the upper hand.’" It would have instead been said: "Do not fear; We will not let them disperse until you throw the staff."

In any case, it appears from his feeling fear within himself that they displayed magic to the people that resembled his miraculous sign or came close to it, even though what they brought was magic with no reality, while what he brought was a miraculous sign (آية معجزة) possessing reality. Allah magnified their magic when He said: فَلَمَّا أَلْقَوْا سَحَرُوا أَعْيُنَ النَّاسِ وَاسْتَرْهَبُوهُمْ وَجاؤُ بِسِحْرٍ عَظِيمٍ "When they threw, they bewitched the people’s eyes and overawed them, and produced a tremendous magic" (7:116). Therefore, Allah supported him here with that which leaves no ambiguity for any onlooker whatsoever: the staff swallowing all that they had bewitched.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْنا لا تَخَفْ إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْأَعْلى "We said, ‘Do not be afraid. Indeed you will have the upper hand.’" (20:68) until His saying: حَيْثُ أَتى "...wherever he may come" (20:69). This is a prohibition for the purpose of strengthening and support. He justified it by His saying: "Indeed you will have the upper hand" (إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْأَعْلى). The meaning is: you are above them from every aspect, and if that is so, nothing of their stratagem or magic will harm you; thus, there is no reason for you to fear.

His saying: "And throw what is in your right hand; it will swallow what they have faked" (وَأَلْقِ ما فِي يَمِينِكَ تَلْقَفْ ما صَنَعُوا) etc., is a command to throw the staff so that it becomes a serpent and swallows what they produced through magic. The expression for the staff as "what is in your right hand" is among the most subtle and profound expressions. It contains an indication that a thing has no reality except what Allah wills. If He wills for what is in the right hand to be a staff, it is a staff; and if He wills for it to be a serpent, it is a serpent. It has nothing of itself. Then, the expression for their snakes and serpents as "what they have faked" (ما صَنَعُوا) indicates that the competition is taking place between that absolute power—which things follow in their names and realities—and this human production, which is nothing but a void stratagem. The word of Allah is the highest, and Allah is dominant over His affair; thus, it is not appropriate for him to fear.

In this sentence—namely His saying: "And throw what is in your right hand; it will swallow what they have faked"—is an explanation of his (peace be upon him) being "higher" according to the outward sense, just as its end is an explanation of his being "higher" according to the reality. For there is no reality to falsehood (الباطل); thus, whoever is upon the Truth (الحق) should not fear falsehood against his Truth.

His saying: "What they have faked is only a magician’s stratagem, and the magician does not prosper wherever he may come" (إِنَّما صَنَعُوا كَيْدُ ساحِرٍ وَلا يُفْلِحُ السَّاحِرُ حَيْثُ أَتى) is a linguistic justification for His saying: "it will swallow what they have faked." The "what" (ma) is either a verbal noun or a relative pronoun. It is a reality-based explanation for his (peace be upon him) being higher, because what they have is a magician’s stratagem with no reality, while what he has is a miraculous sign possessing reality; and the Truth rises and is not risen over.

His saying: "and the magician does not prosper wherever he may come" (وَلا يُفْلِحُ السَّاحِرُ حَيْثُ أَتى) serves as the major premise (الكبرى) for His saying: "What they have faked is only a magician’s stratagem." For what the magician attains through his magic is a void imagination in the onlookers with no reality; there is no prosperity (فلاح) or true happiness to be gained in an illusory matter with no reality.

Thus, His saying: "and the magician does not prosper wherever he may come" is similar to His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ "...Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (6:144) and وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ "...and Allah does not guide the transgressing people" (5:108), and others. All are branches of: إِنَّ الْباطِلَ كانَ زَهُوقاً "...Indeed falsehood is bound to vanish" (17:81) and وَيَمْحُ اللهُ الْباطِلَ وَيُحِقُّ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ "...Allah will efface falsehood and confirm the truth by His words" (42:24). Falsehood continues to adorn matters and make them resemble the Truth, and the Truth continues to efface it and swallow what it displayed to the imagination of the onlookers, sooner or later. The example of Moses' staff and the magic of the magicians applies to every falsehood that appears and every Truth that swallows and vanishes it. A beneficial discussion on this matter has passed in the commentary of His saying: أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَسالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِها "He sends down water from the sky, and the valleys flow according to their capacity..." (13:17).

His saying, the Exalted: فَأُلْقِيَ السَّحَرَةُ سُجَّداً قالُوا آمَنَّا بِرَبِّ هارُونَ وَمُوسى "Thereat the magicians fell down prostrating. They said, ‘We believe in the Lord of Aaron and Moses.’" (20:70). In the speech is an omission and brevity; the estimation is: "So he threw what was in his right hand, and it swallowed what they had faked, and thereat the magicians were cast down." In the expression "the magicians were cast down" (فَأُلْقِيَ السَّحَرَةُ)—using the passive voice—rather than saying "the magicians prostrated," there is an indication of the divine power humbling them and the Truth overwhelming them by its manifestation, such that they found no choice but to fall to the ground prostrating, as if they had no will in that. Rather, an "Other" cast them down without them knowing who He was.

Their saying: "We believe in the Lord of Aaron and Moses" (آمَنَّا بِرَبِّ هارُونَ وَمُوسى) is their testimony of faith. They attributed Him, the Exalted, to Moses and Aaron so that it would contain testimony to His lordship and the messengership of both Moses and Aaron. The sentence "They said..." etc., is separate without a conjunction because it is like a response to a hypothetical question: "What did they say?" It was said: "They said..." etc.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ آمَنْتُمْ لَهُ قَبْلَ أَنْ آذَنَ لَكُمْ "He said, ‘Do you believe in him before I give you leave?’" (20:71) until the end of the verse. "The chief" (الكبير) is the leader. "Cutting off hands and feet on opposite sides" (قطع الأيدي والأرجل من خلاف) means cutting the right hand and the left foot. "Crucifying" (التصليب) denotes the frequency and intensity of crucifixion, like "cutting" (التقطيع) which denotes frequency and intensity of cutting. "Trunks" (الجذوع) is the plural of jidh', which is the trunk of the palm tree.

His saying: "Do you believe in him before I give you leave?" is a threat from Pharaoh when they believed. The sentence is interrogative with the particle omitted, and the interrogation is for denunciation; or it is declarative intended to establish the crime. His saying: "He is indeed your chief who has taught you magic" (إِنَّهُ لَكَبِيرُكُمُ الَّذِي عَلَّمَكُمُ السِّحْرَ) is an accusation of a political conspiracy against Coptic society in the land of Egypt. It is as if they conspired with their chief that Moses would claim prophethood and call the people of Egypt to Allah, bringing magic for that purpose, and they would be sought for help against him. Then, when they attended and gathered to compete with him, they would fail, be defeated by him, and believe; the common folk would follow them, their "excellent tradition" would vanish from among them, and those who did not believe would be expelled. Allah said in another place: إِنَّ هذا لَمَكْرٌ مَكَرْتُمُوهُ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ لِتُخْرِجُوا مِنْها أَهْلَها "This is indeed a plot you have hatched in the city to expel its people from it" (7:123). He only threw this statement at them to incite the common folk against them, just as he had accused Moses (peace be upon him) of the same on the first day.

His saying: "I will surely cut off your hands and feet on opposite sides..." until the end of the verse, is a promise and threat of severe punishment. Allah did not mention in His speech whether he carried that out against them or not.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا لَنْ نُؤْثِرَكَ عَلى ما جاءَنا مِنَ الْبَيِّناتِ وَالَّذِي فَطَرَنا فَاقْضِ ما أَنْتَ قاضٍ إِنَّما تَقْضِي هذِهِ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا "They said, ‘We will never prefer you to the manifest proofs that have come to us and [to] Him who created us. So decide whatever you will decide. You can only decide about the life of this world.’" (20:72). This is a speech eloquent in its wording, profound in its concept, far-reaching in its meaning, and exalted in its station. It boils and overflows with knowledge and wisdom. These were a people who, an hour before, had their hearts filled with the awe and majesty of Pharaoh, and their souls humbled by the adornments and trappings of the world he possessed—which are nothing but lies of the imagination and vanities of the mind. They used to call him the "exalted lord" and said when they threw their ropes and staffs: "By the might of Pharaoh, we shall indeed be the victors!" Yet, no sooner did the signs of the Truth appear to them than their eyes were dazzled, and the might and authority they saw in Pharaoh, and the value and status of the world's adornments he possessed, vanished. Their hearts were overwhelmed, removing from them the vices of cowardice, flattery, following whims, and infatuation with the mirage of the life of this world. In their place, they established attachment to the Truth, entry under the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah, and pride in His might. Thus, they desire nothing but what Allah desires, they hope in none but Allah, and they fear none but Allah, mighty is His Name.

All of this becomes apparent through contemplation (التدبر) of the ongoing dialogue between Pharaoh and the magicians when their respective statements are compared. Pharaoh, in a state of heedlessness (غفلة) regarding the station of his Lord, sees nothing but himself. He attributes to himself that he is the lord of the Copts, that the kingdom of Egypt belongs to him, that he possesses mobilized armies, and that he has whatever he desires and decrees. In the view of Truth and Reality (الحق والحقيقة), these are nothing but empty claims. His ignorance has deluded him to the point where he perceives Truth as a follower of his falsehood, and Reality as being subject and obedient to his claim. Thus, he expects that the souls of the people—which are inherently disposed toward understanding and judgment—should not perceive or sense anything through their innate perception (الإدراك الجبلي) except after his permission, nor should their hearts submit or attain certainty in any truth except by his authorization. This is expressed in his saying to the magicians: آمَنْتُمْ لَهُ قَبْلَ أَنْ آذَنَ لَكُمْ "Do you believe in him before I give you leave?" (20:71).

He believes that there is no reality to man except this physical structure (البنية الجسمانية) which lives and then decays and perishes, and that there is no felicity (سعادة) for him except in attaining these fleeting material pleasures. This is reflected in his saying: فَلَأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُمْ مِنْ خِلافٍ وَلَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ فِي جُذُوعِ النَّخْلِ وَلَتَعْلَمُنَّ أَيُّنا أَشَدُّ عَذاباً وَأَبْقى "I will surely cut off your hands and feet on opposite sides, and I will surely crucify you on the trunks of palm trees, and you will surely know which of us is severer in punishment and more abiding" (20:71). One should also contemplate the conclusion of his speech.

As for these believers, once the Truth (الحق) reached them and enveloped them, He purified them and made them sincere for Himself. Consequently, they viewed what Pharaoh considered a reality—the trappings of the life of this world (الحياة الدنيا) such as wealth and status—as an illusory mirage (سراب خيالي) and a deceptive, false adornment. When they were given the choice between these things and what they had believed in, they were effectively choosing between Truth and falsehood, and between Reality and a mirage. Far be it from the people of certainty (أهل اليقين) to doubt their certainty or to prefer falsehood over Truth and a mirage over Reality, while they witness it with ocular witnessing (شهادة عيان). This is expressed in their saying: لَنْ نُؤْثِرَكَ—meaning, we will not choose you—عَلى ما جاءَنا مِنَ الْبَيِّناتِ وَالَّذِي فَطَرَنا "We will never prefer you to the manifest proofs that have come to us and to Him who created us" (20:72). Their intention was not merely to prefer a person as a human body possessed of a soul, but rather to reject what he possessed and claimed to own of the vast world with its wealth and attainments.

Regarding Pharaoh's threat of a gruesome death, severe torment, and the severing of the ties of worldly life—he believing that man has nothing but this life in which his felicity and wretchedness reside—they viewed the matter in the opposite light. They saw that man possesses an eternal, everlasting life (حياة خالدة أبدية) compared to which this fleeting, immediate life has no value. If one attains felicity therein, it matters not if he suffers in his worldly life; and if he is wretched therein, nothing will avail him.

Accordingly, they did not fear losing their perishing worldly life if they gained the eternal Hereafter. This is found in their saying to Pharaoh—which was a response to his threat of execution: فَاقْضِ ما أَنْتَ قاضٍ إِنَّما تَقْضِي هذِهِ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا "So decide whatever you will decide. You can only decide regarding the life of this world" (20:72). The subsequent verses recounting the remainder of their speech to Pharaoh serve as a justification and clarification for their saying: "We will never prefer you to the manifest proofs that have come to us and to Him who created us."

In their saying: ما جاءَنا مِنَ الْبَيِّناتِ "the manifest proofs (البينات) — the clear and undeniable signs that establish the truth — that have come to us," there is an allusion (تلويح) that they considered what they witnessed regarding the staff to be numerous signs (آيات), such as its becoming a serpent, its swallowing the ropes and staffs, and its returning a second time to its original state. It is also possible that the word "from" (مِنَ) denotes partitivity (التبعيض), implying they witnessed one sign and believed that Allah possesses many other signs, though this interpretation is somewhat remote.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا آمَنَّا بِرَبِّنا لِيَغْفِرَ لَنا خَطايانا وَما أَكْرَهْتَنا عَلَيْهِ مِنَ السِّحْرِ وَاللهُ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقى "We have indeed believed in our Lord that He may forgive us our sins and the magic you compelled us to perform. Allah is better and more lasting" (20:73). The sins (الخطايا) is the plural of khati'ah (خطيئة), which is close in meaning to "evil deed" (السيئة). His saying: وَما أَكْرَهْتَنا عَلَيْهِ "and what you compelled us to perform" is conjoined to "our sins," and مِنَ السِّحْرِ "of magic" is an explanation (بيان) for it. The meaning is: "that He may forgive us the magic you compelled us to perform." This indicates that they were coerced into it, either when they were gathered to Pharaoh from their homes or when they disputed their affair among themselves and kept their counsel secret, whereupon they were forced into the confrontation and competition.

The beginning of the verse is a justification for their saying: "We will never prefer you..." etc. That is: "We have only chosen Allah, who created us, over you and believed in Him so that He may forgive us our sins and the magic you compelled us to perform." The conclusion of the verse: وَاللهُ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقى "Allah is better and more lasting" completes the clarification and serves as a justification for the beginning of the verse, as if it were said: "We have only preferred His forgiveness over your favor because He is better and more lasting"—meaning better than every good and more lasting than everything that remains, due to the absolute nature (الإطلاق) of the expression—so nothing can be preferred over Him. This conclusion also serves as a counter-point to the conclusion of Pharaoh's speech: "and you will surely know which of us is severer in punishment and more abiding."

They referred to Him, the Exalted, first as "He who created us" (الذي فطرنا), second as "our Lord" (ربنا), and third as "Allah" (الله). As for the first, it is because His being the Creator (فاطر) — the One who brings us out from the concealment of non-existence into existence — implies that every true good terminates in Him, and that anything else, when compared to Him, is nothing but a mirage of falsehood (سراب البطلان). This is the basis for all preference (ترجيح), and the context here is one of preferring Him, the Exalted, over Pharaoh.

As for the second, it is because it reports their faith (الإيمان) in Him, and the attribute of Allah most closely related to faith and servitude (العبودية) is the attribute of His Lordship (الربوبية), which encompasses the meaning of ownership (الملك) and governance (التدبير).

As for the third, it is because the criterion for something being "good" is perfection (الكمال), and with Allah, the Exalted, reside all the attributes of perfection that necessitate His absolute goodness. Thus, it was appropriate to use the Proper Name (العلم) which denotes the Essence (الذات) that integrates all attributes of perfection. Accordingly, the speech in these three instances, despite its apparent simplicity, contains the proof (الحجة) for the claim. The true meaning is: "We will never prefer you over He who created us, because He created us; and we have believed in our Lord because He is our Lord; and Allah is better because He is Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه)."

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ مَنْ يَأْتِ رَبَّهُ مُجْرِماً فَإِنَّ لَهُ جَهَنَّمَ لا يَمُوتُ فِيها وَلا يَحْيى "Whoever comes to his Lord as a guilty one, for him there shall be hell, wherein he will neither die nor live" (20:74). This is a justification for making the forgiveness of sins the goal of faith in Allah; that is, because whoever's sins are not forgiven is a criminal (مجرم), and "whoever comes to his Lord as a criminal..." etc.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَأْتِهِ مُؤْمِناً قَدْ عَمِلَ الصَّالِحاتِ فَأُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الدَّرَجاتُ الْعُلى "But whoever comes to Him as a believer, having done righteous deeds—for such there will be high stations" (20:75) until the end of the following verse. The station (الدرجة)—according to what al-Raghib mentioned—is a rank (المنزلة), but it implies ascent (الصعود), like the steps of a ladder. Its opposite is the "descending rank" (الدركة), which is a rank of descent (حدورا). For this reason, one speaks of the "levels" (درجات) of Paradise and the "depths" (دركات) of the Fire. Purification (التزكي) is to grow through righteous growth (النماء الصالح), and it refers to a person living with true belief and righteous deeds (عمل صالح).

The two verses describe the consequences of faith and righteous deeds, just as the preceding verse described the consequences of criminality (الاجرام) resulting from disbelief or disobedience. These three verses describing the consequences of criminality and faith are directed at Pharaoh's threat and his promise to them. Pharaoh had threatened them for their faith in Moses with amputation and crucifixion, claiming it was the severest and most abiding punishment. They countered this by stating that for the criminal, with his Lord, is Hell, wherein he neither dies nor lives. He does not die so as to be saved from enduring the pain of its torment—whereas the ultimate end of worldly punishment is death, in which the tormented criminal finds escape—nor does he live therein, for it contains nothing that makes life pleasant, nor is there any hoped-for good for which one might endure torment in anticipation.

Pharaoh had previously promised them status by making them among those brought near (المقربين) and a reward (الأجر), as Allah narrated: قالُوا إِنَّ لَنا لَأَجْراً إِنْ كُنَّا نَحْنُ الْغالِبِينَ قالَ نَعَمْ وَإِنَّكُمْ لَمِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ "They said, 'Shall we have a reward if we are the victors?' He said, 'Yes, and you will indeed be among those brought near [to me]'" (7:113-114). They countered this by stating that whoever comes to Him as a believer having done righteous deeds—and the use of the distant demonstrative "those" (أُولئِكَ) is to magnify their status—for them are the high stations (الدرجات العلى), which corresponds to Pharaoh's promise of being "brought near." جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها ذلِكَ جَزاءُ مَنْ تَزَكَّى "the Gardens of Eden, with rivers flowing flowing at their feet, to remain in them [forever]. That is the reward of him who purifies himself" (20:76)—through faith and righteous deeds—and this corresponds to his promise of a "reward."

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَوْحَيْنا إِلى مُوسى أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبادِي فَاضْرِبْ لَهُمْ طَرِيقاً فِي الْبَحْرِ يَبَساً "We revealed to Moses, [saying], 'Set out with My servants at night, and strike out for them a dry path in the sea...'" (20:77) until His saying: وَما هَدَى "and he did not lead [them] aright" (20:79). Setting out at night (الإسراء) is traveling by night, and "My servants" refers to the Children of Israel. Regarding His saying: فَاضْرِبْ لَهُمْ طَرِيقاً فِي الْبَحْرِ يَبَساً "and strike out for them a dry path in the sea," it is said that "striking" (الضرب) refers to striking with the staff, as indicated by His speech in other places, and that "path" (طريقاً) is the direct object of "strike" (أضرب) by way of extension (الاتساع), which is a rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي); the original meaning being "strike the sea so that it becomes a path for them." [End of quote]. It is also possible that "striking" (الضرب) here means constructing or establishing, as in the expressions "I pitched (ضربت) the tent" or "I established (ضربت) the foundation."

Dry (اليبس)—according to what al-Raghib mentioned—is a place that once contained water which then departed. Overtaking (الدرك) refers to the consequence (تبعة) of a thing. In the attribution of "enveloping" (الغشيان) to the ambiguous relative pronoun "what" (ما) and making it the relative clause (صلة) as well, there is a representation of the horror of the situation that is not hidden. It is said that in His saying: وَأَضَلَّ فِرْعَوْنُ قَوْمَهُ وَما هَدَى "Pharaoh led his people astray and he did not lead [them] aright" (20:79), there is a refutation of Pharaoh's statement to his people when he addressed them: وَما أَهْدِيكُمْ إِلَّا سَبِيلَ الرَّشادِ "I only guide you to the way of rectitude" (40:29). Based on this, His saying: وَما هَدَى "and he did not lead [them] aright" is not a mere emphasis or repetition of the meaning of His saying: "Pharaoh led his people astray."

(Narrative Discussion)

In Nahj al-Balagha, he (peace be upon him) said: "Moses did not harbor fear for his own sake; rather, he feared the triumph of the ignorant and the dominion of misguidance."

I say: Its meaning is what we have previously presented in the commentary (تفسير) of the verse.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Jundub ibn Abdillah al-Bajali who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "When you catch a magician, kill him." Then he recited: وَلا يُفْلِحُ السَّاحِرُ حَيْثُ أَتى "and the magician does not succeed wherever he may come" (20:69). He said: "He shall not be safe wherever he is found."

I say: The correspondence of the meaning mentioned in the hadith to the verse, given its context (سياق), is somewhat obscure.

Ayahs

يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ قَدْ أَنجَيْنَـٰكُم مِّنْ عَدُوِّكُمْ وَوَٰعَدْنَـٰكُمْ جَانِبَ ٱلطُّورِ ٱلْأَيْمَنَ وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَنَّ وَٱلسَّلْوَىٰ (٨٠)
O Children of Israel! We delivered you from your enemy, and We appointed with you a tryst on the right side of the Mount and We sent down to you manna and quails: (80)
كُلُوا۟ مِن طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَا رَزَقْنَـٰكُمْ وَلَا تَطْغَوْا۟ فِيهِ فَيَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبِى ۖ وَمَن يَحْلِلْ عَلَيْهِ غَضَبِى فَقَدْ هَوَىٰ (٨١)
‘Eat of the good things We have provided you, but do not overstep the bounds therein, lest My wrath should descend on you, and he on whom My wrath descends certainly perishes. (81)
وَإِنِّى لَغَفَّارٌ لِّمَن تَابَ وَءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا ثُمَّ ٱهْتَدَىٰ (٨٢)
Indeed I forgive those who repent, become faithful, act righteously, and follow guidance.’ (82)
۞ وَمَآ أَعْجَلَكَ عَن قَوْمِكَ يَـٰمُوسَىٰ (٨٣)
[God said,] ‘O Moses, what has prompted you to hasten ahead of your people?’ (83)
قَالَ هُمْ أُو۟لَآءِ عَلَىٰٓ أَثَرِى وَعَجِلْتُ إِلَيْكَ رَبِّ لِتَرْضَىٰ (٨٤)
He said, ‘They are close upon my heels, and I hurried on to You, my Lord, that You may be pleased.’ (84)
قَالَ فَإِنَّا قَدْ فَتَنَّا قَوْمَكَ مِنۢ بَعْدِكَ وَأَضَلَّهُمُ ٱلسَّامِرِىُّ (٨٥)
He said, ‘Indeed We tried your people in your absence, and the Samiri has led them astray.’ (85)
فَرَجَعَ مُوسَىٰٓ إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ غَضْبَـٰنَ أَسِفًا ۚ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَلَمْ يَعِدْكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ وَعْدًا حَسَنًا ۚ أَفَطَالَ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْعَهْدُ أَمْ أَرَدتُّمْ أَن يَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ فَأَخْلَفْتُم مَّوْعِدِى (٨٦)
Thereupon Moses returned to his people, indignant and grieved. He said, ‘O my people! Did your Lord not give you a true promise? Did the period [of my absence] seem too long to you? Or did you desire that your Lord’s wrath should descend on you and so you failed your tryst with me?’ (86)
قَالُوا۟ مَآ أَخْلَفْنَا مَوْعِدَكَ بِمَلْكِنَا وَلَـٰكِنَّا حُمِّلْنَآ أَوْزَارًا مِّن زِينَةِ ٱلْقَوْمِ فَقَذَفْنَـٰهَا فَكَذَٰلِكَ أَلْقَى ٱلسَّامِرِىُّ (٨٧)
They said, ‘We did not fail our tryst with you of our own accord, but we were laden with the weight of those people’s ornaments, and we cast them [into the fire] and so did the Samiri.’ (87)
فَأَخْرَجَ لَهُمْ عِجْلًا جَسَدًا لَّهُۥ خُوَارٌ فَقَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمْ وَإِلَـٰهُ مُوسَىٰ فَنَسِىَ (٨٨)
Then he produced for them a calf—a [lifeless] body with a low—and they said, This is your god and the god of Moses, so he forgot! (88)
أَفَلَا يَرَوْنَ أَلَّا يَرْجِعُ إِلَيْهِمْ قَوْلًا وَلَا يَمْلِكُ لَهُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا (٨٩)
Did they not see that it did not answer them, nor could it bring them any benefit or harm? (89)
وَلَقَدْ قَالَ لَهُمْ هَـٰرُونُ مِن قَبْلُ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِنَّمَا فُتِنتُم بِهِۦ ۖ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ فَٱتَّبِعُونِى وَأَطِيعُوٓا۟ أَمْرِى (٩٠)
Aaron had certainly told them earlier, ‘O my people! You are only being tested by it. Indeed your Lord is the All-beneficent. So follow me and obey my command!’ (90)
قَالُوا۟ لَن نَّبْرَحَ عَلَيْهِ عَـٰكِفِينَ حَتَّىٰ يَرْجِعَ إِلَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ (٩١)
They had said, ‘We will keep on clinging to it until Moses returns to us.’ (91)
قَالَ يَـٰهَـٰرُونُ مَا مَنَعَكَ إِذْ رَأَيْتَهُمْ ضَلُّوٓا۟ (٩٢)
He said, ‘O Aaron! What kept you, when you saw them going astray, (92)
أَلَّا تَتَّبِعَنِ ۖ أَفَعَصَيْتَ أَمْرِى (٩٣)
from following me? Did you disobey my command?’ (93)
قَالَ يَبْنَؤُمَّ لَا تَأْخُذْ بِلِحْيَتِى وَلَا بِرَأْسِىٓ ۖ إِنِّى خَشِيتُ أَن تَقُولَ فَرَّقْتَ بَيْنَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَلَمْ تَرْقُبْ قَوْلِى (٩٤)
He said, ‘O son of my mother! Do not grab my beard or my head! I feared lest you should say, ‘‘You have caused a rift among the Children of Israel and did not heed my word [of advice].’’’ (94)
قَالَ فَمَا خَطْبُكَ يَـٰسَـٰمِرِىُّ (٩٥)
He said, ‘What is your business, O Samiri?’ (95)
قَالَ بَصُرْتُ بِمَا لَمْ يَبْصُرُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَقَبَضْتُ قَبْضَةً مِّنْ أَثَرِ ٱلرَّسُولِ فَنَبَذْتُهَا وَكَذَٰلِكَ سَوَّلَتْ لِى نَفْسِى (٩٦)
He said, ‘I saw what they did not see. I took a handful [of dust] from the messenger’s trail and threw it. That is how my soul prompted me.’ (96)
قَالَ فَٱذْهَبْ فَإِنَّ لَكَ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ أَن تَقُولَ لَا مِسَاسَ ۖ وَإِنَّ لَكَ مَوْعِدًا لَّن تُخْلَفَهُۥ ۖ وَٱنظُرْ إِلَىٰٓ إِلَـٰهِكَ ٱلَّذِى ظَلْتَ عَلَيْهِ عَاكِفًا ۖ لَّنُحَرِّقَنَّهُۥ ثُمَّ لَنَنسِفَنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ نَسْفًا (٩٧)
He said, ‘Begone! It shall be your [lot] throughout life to say, ‘‘Do not touch me!’’ Indeed, there is a tryst for you which you will not fail to keep! Now look at your god to whom you went on clinging. We will burn it down and then scatter it[s ashes] into the sea. (97)
إِنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًا (٩٨)
Indeed your God is Allah. There is no god except Him. He embraces all things in [His] knowledge.’ (98)

(Commentary)

The final section of the story of Moses (عليه السلام) presented in this Surah recounts several of the favors Allah bestowed upon the Children of Israel, such as saving them from their enemy, the appointment at the right side of Mount Tur (الطُّور), and the sending down of manna and quails. It concludes by mentioning the story of the Samiri and his leading the people astray through the worship of the calf—a story connected to the appointment at Mount Tur.

This part of the section—which contains an explanation of the Children of Israel exposing themselves to the wrath of the Exalted—is the primary objective of this chapter. For this reason, the discourse details it extensively, while other matters are mentioned only through brief allusion and summary.

His saying, the Exalted: يا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ قَدْ أَنْجَيْناكُمْ مِنْ عَدُوِّكُمْ — "O Children of Israel, We delivered you from your enemy" (20:80) to the end of the verse. It is as if the speech implies a preceding command of "saying," meaning: We said, "O Children of Israel..." His saying: قَدْ أَنْجَيْناكُمْ مِنْ عَدُوِّكُمْ ("We delivered you from your enemy") refers to Pharaoh, whom Allah drowned, thereby saving the Children of Israel from him after a long period of tribulation.

His saying: وَواعَدْناكُمْ جانِبَ الطُّورِ الْأَيْمَنَ — "and We made an appointment with you at the right side of the Mount" (20:80). The word "right" (أَيْمَنَ) is in the accusative case as an adjective for "side" (جانِب). Perhaps what is meant by this appointment is the forty-night appointment of Moses for the revelation of the Torah. This story has already been discussed in Surah al-Baqarah and elsewhere, as has the story of the sending down of manna and quails.

His saying: كُلُوا مِنْ طَيِّباتِ ما رَزَقْناكُمْ — "Eat of the good things We have provided you" (20:81) is a permission (إباحة) expressed in the form of a command. The annexation of "good things" (طَيِّباتِ) to "what We have provided you" (ما رَزَقْناكُمْ) is the annexation of an adjective to the noun it describes. This is because it would be meaningless for Allah to attribute provision to Himself and then divide it into "good" and "not good," as supported by His saying in another place: وَرَزَقْناهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ — "and provided them with good things" (45:16).

His saying: وَلا تَطْغَوْا فِيهِ فَيَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبِي — "and do not overstep the bounds therein, lest My wrath should descend upon you" (20:81). The pronoun "therein" (فِيهِ) refers to the act of eating related to the "good things." This overstepping occurs through ingratitude for the blessing and failing to give thanks, as they said: يا مُوسى لَنْ نَصْبِرَ عَلى طَعامٍ واحِدٍ فَادْعُ لَنا رَبَّكَ يُخْرِجْ لَنا مِمَّا تُنْبِتُ الْأَرْضُ مِنْ بَقْلِها وَقِثَّائِها وَفُومِها وَعَدَسِها وَبَصَلِها — "O Moses, we will not put up with one kind of food. So invoke your Lord for us, so that He may bring forth for us of what the earth grows—its greens and its cucumbers, its garlic, its lentils, and its onions" (2:61).

His saying: فَيَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبِي ("lest My wrath should descend upon you") means My wrath becomes due and necessary. It is derived from the "falling due" (حُلول) of a debt (الدين), which is said to "fall due" (يحل) when its repayment becomes obligatory. Wrath (الغضب), among the active attributes (صفاته الفعلية) of the Exalted, is manifested as His will to inflict harm upon a servant by preparing the causes for that as a result of a sin committed.

His saying: وَمَنْ يَحْلِلْ عَلَيْهِ غَضَبِي فَقَدْ هَوى — "and he on whom My wrath descends certainly perishes" (20:81). That is, he falls, from the word al-huwi (الهوي) meaning to fall, and it has been interpreted as destruction (الهلاك).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنِّي لَغَفَّارٌ لِمَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً ثُمَّ اهْتَدى — "Indeed I am most forgiving toward him who repents, believes, and acts righteously, and then follows guidance" (20:82). This is a promise of emphasized mercy following a severe warning. For this reason, He described Himself with the intensive form of forgiveness, saying: وَإِنِّي لَغَفَّارٌ ("Indeed I am most forgiving"), rather than saying "I am the Forgiver" or "I shall forgive."

Repentance (التوبة), which is "returning" (الرجوع), can be from sin to obedience, just as it can be from polytheism (الشرك) to monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. Similarly, faith (الإيمان) can be in Allah, just as it can be in the signs (آيات) of Allah, including His prophets, messengers, and every ruling they brought from Allah the Exalted. The use of "faith" in the Quran is frequent in both senses, as is the use of "repentance" in both aforementioned senses. The Children of Israel were both embroiled in sins through which they became transgressors (فسقوا) and embroiled in polytheism, such as the worship of the calf. Therefore, there is no reason to divert the speech from its apparent generality regarding repentance from both polytheism and sin, and faith in Allah and His signs. Likewise, it remains general regarding the repentant and the believers among the Children of Israel and others, even if the Children of Israel were the immediate subjects of the address; for divine attributes like forgiveness are not restricted to one people over another.

Thus, the meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: "I am indeed abundantly forgiving to every human being who repents and believes, whether they repent from polytheism or from sin, and whether they believe in Me or in My signs through My messengers or the rulings they brought, by regretting what they did and performing righteous deeds (عملاً صالحاً) by replacing opposition and rebellion in what they disobeyed with obedience therein." This fulfills the essential meaning of "returning" from something. A detailed discussion on this was provided in the commentary on His saying: إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللهِ — "Repentance [accepted] by Allah is only for..." (4:17) in the fourth volume of this book.

As for His saying: ثُمَّ اهْتَدى ("and then follows guidance"), guidance (الاهتداء) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — stands in contrast to straying (الضلال), as evidenced by His saying: مَنِ اهْتَدى فَإِنَّما يَهْتَدِي لِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ ضَلَّ فَإِنَّما يَضِلُّ عَلَيْها — "Whoever is guided is only guided for [the good of] his own soul, and whoever goes astray, only goes astray to its detriment" (17:15), and His saying: لا يَضُرُّكُمْ مَنْ ضَلَّ إِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ — "he who errs cannot harm you if you are guided" (5:105). Does it mean that he should not stray in the very matter he repented from by returning to the sin again—thereby implying that repentance for a sin only benefits what was committed before the repentance and does not suffice if he returns to it a second time? Or does it mean that he should not stray in other matters—thereby implying that forgiveness only benefits him regarding the sin he repented for if he does not stray in other deeds? Or is it intended to encompass both meanings?

The apparent use of the conjunction "then" (ثُمَّ) suggests the first meaning, implying steadfastness (الثبات) and uprightness (الاستقامة) in repentance. This returns to the condition of "rectification" (الإصلاح) mentioned in several verses, such as His saying: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — "excepting those who repent after that and reform, for Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful" (3:89, 24:5).

However, two issues remain with the verse under this interpretation: First, the point of using the intensive form "Most Forgiving" (الغفار), which denotes abundance. What is the meaning of the abundance of His forgiveness for someone who committed a single sin and then repented?

Second, its implication would be that whoever violates one of His rulings is a disbeliever in Him, even if he acknowledges that it is from Allah and only disobeys out of following passion (اتباع الهوى) rather than rejecting the ruling—unless it is said that the verse, by including the phrase "repents and believes" (تابَ وَآمَنَ), only includes the polytheist or the one who rejects a divine ruling, which is problematic as you can see.

Thus, it can be said: Repentance and faith here mean repentance from polytheism and faith in Allah, as these are the two meanings intended in most places in His speech where repentance and faith are mentioned together. Accordingly, the meaning of His saying: وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً ("and acts righteously") would be obedience to His rulings by complying with His commands and refraining from His prohibitions. The meaning of the verse would then be: "Whoever repents from polytheism, believes in Allah, and performs the duties He has obligated, I am abundantly forgiving of his misdeeds; I forgive him slip after slip, so the forgiveness becomes abundant due to the abundance of its instances."

Allah has mentioned a similar meaning—the forgiveness of misdeeds—in His saying: إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ — "If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds" (4:31).

Therefore, His saying: وَإِنِّي لَغَفَّارٌ لِمَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً corresponds to the verse in Surah an-Nisa, but there remains an additional condition that restricts the ruling of forgiveness, which is the meaning of His saying: ثُمَّ اهْتَدى ("and then follows guidance"). This is the "finding of the way" (الاهتداء إلى الطريق). It appears that forgiveness is only granted to the believer who performs righteous deeds if he seeks it through its proper way and enters it through its proper door.

We do not find in His speech anything that restricts faith in Allah and righteous deeds in their effect and acceptance before Allah except faith in the Messenger—in the sense of submission (التسليم) to him, obeying him in matters great and small, taking the religion from him, following the path he outlines, and adhering to him without independence or innovation (ابتداع) that leads to following the footsteps of Satan. In short, it is his guardianship (الولاية) over the believers in their religious and worldly affairs. Allah has legislated his guardianship, made his obedience mandatory, and obligated taking from him and following his example in very many verses—there is no need to cite them all, nor is there space to exhaust them. "The Prophet is closer to the believers than their own selves."

Most of the Children of Israel, despite their faith in Allah and their belief in the messengership of Moses and Aaron, were hesitant regarding their guardianship—or acted as if they were hesitant—as is explicit in the general accounts of them in the Book of Allah.
